tasks.title: Wedding Signage for Print (high res, not AI generated)
tasks.task_type: Wedding Signage Family — welcome, seating chart, menu, program, table signs (print-ready)
tasks.one_line_task: Design a cohesive six-piece print-ready wedding signage family in a soft-floral direction that coordinates with — but never alters — the couple's beloved signature-drinks sign.
tasks.one_line_outputs: Six print pieces at exact trim sizes — welcome signs, menu/thank-you card, seating chart, program and table signs 1–6 — as 300-DPI files plus a combined print PDF.
tasks.brief: Applicants, please share examples of similar work in your proposal. Hello! I love the attached signature drinks image I created and would like to keep that sign as is. However, the other signs do not coordinate well with it, and they are not high enough resolution for professional print quality. The current backgrounds were AI-generated, which limits the DPI and makes them risky for large-format printing. I’m looking to create a cohesive new family of signs in print-ready resolution. The attached seating chart and welcome sign drafts show the general sign types that need to be redesigned. Creative direction: Option 1: Continue with the soft floral theme. The flowers should be inspired by this arrangement: https://bifloralsilkflowers.com/producto/emma/arch-arrangement-6/ Option 2: Use a more elevated black script direction, similar to the attached example.jpg. Either way, I’d like the signs to feel like a coordinated family, while allowing the signature drinks sign to remain the more playful, personal piece. Files needed: 1. Welcome Gathering Sign Size: 20 in x 30 in Minimum resolution at 300 DPI: 6000 x 9000 pixels 2. Welcome Wedding Ceremony & Reception Sign Size: 35.4 in x 23.6 in Minimum resolution at 300 DPI: 10,620 x 7,080 pixels 3. Menu / Thank You Long Card 4 in × 9 in, portrait orientation, with 0.125 in bleed on all sides. Minimum export resolution should be 1200 × 2700 pixels at final trim size, 4. Seating Chart Size: 47.2 in x 35.4 in Minimum resolution at 300 DPI: 14,160 x 10,620 pixels (I'll provide the name lists soon) 5. Wedding Program Size: 5 x 7 folded, 10 x 7 flat Bleed: 0.125 in on all sides Safe margin: 0.25 in Resolution: 300 DPI minimum 6. Table signs for tables 1-6 5 x 7 inches Resolution: 300 DPI minimum Please provide the final files as print-ready PDFs, preferably with vector text and artwork where possible, plus editable source files. Please also include bleed if required by the printer, ideally 0.125 in to 0.25 in, and keep all important text safely inside the trim area. Thank you!
tasks.mcp_workflow: search_design (Express wedding signage templates: welcome / seating chart / menu / program / table number) -> fill_text (couple names, copy, menu, table assignments) -> asset_add_file (floral / black-script reference) -> image_crop_and_resize (each exact trim size) -> document_convert_pdf (print-ready PDF w/ bleed)

tasks.readme:
# Wedding Signage Family — Hartwell Wedding (Task 5366)

Six-piece coordinated print suite, **Option 1: soft floral**, built to `print_spec.json`
exactly. Palette: ivory `#FBF7EF` fields, blush `#EAC9C1` + sage `#9CAF88` accents, all
lettering charcoal `#3B3B38`. Typography: Snell Roundhand script + Hoefler Text serif
(regular/italic, letterspaced caps). Every floral element derives from your
`floral_reference.jpg`, processed through real Adobe connector operations
(auto-tone -> background removal -> smart 1:1 crop -> generative expand).
**Your `signature_drinks_sign.png` was never altered or re-exported** — verified
byte-identical (md5 `542032a80cf91381032a952126846c41`); the family coordinates with it.

## Asked -> produced

| # | Asked (print_spec.json) | Produced file(s) | Pixels / DPI | How it was made |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Welcome Gathering Sign — 20 x 30 in, min 6000 x 9000 px @300 | `01_welcome_gathering_20x30.png` | 6000 x 9000 @300 | Arch cutout (connector remove-background) bleeding top-right + mirrored 55% copy bottom-left; gathering wording verbatim — names line in script, remaining lines letterspaced caps |
| 2 | Welcome Ceremony & Reception Sign — 35.4 x 23.6 in, min 10620 x 7080 px @300 | `02_welcome_ceremony_reception_35.4x23.6.png` | 10620 x 7080 @300 | Centered stack (names script + 6px sage hairline, date caps, welcome line italic); arch cutouts on left/right edges; connector generative-expand floral as blurred base band |
| 3 | Menu / Thank You long card — 4 x 9 in + 0.125 in bleed, min 1200 x 2700 px at trim | `03a_menu_thankyou_front_4x9.png` (front), `03b_menu_thankyou_back_4x9.png` (back) | 1275 x 2775 each @300 (= 4.25 x 9.25 in artwork; trim 1200 x 2700 centered; text held >=0.25 in inside trim) | Front: all 3 courses / 6 choices + descriptions verbatim. Back: full thank-you message, names in script, hashtag letterspaced at base, connector-cropped rose-cluster garnish |
| 4 | Seating Chart — 47.2 x 35.4 in, min 14160 x 10620 px @300 | `04_seating_chart_47.2x35.4.png` | 14160 x 10620 @300 | 15 ivory panels (sage hairline border) in a 5x3 grid; all 120 guest names verbatim incl. suffixes, alphabetized order preserved; head-table note as italic footer; florals kept clear of name panels |
| 5 | Wedding Program — 5 x 7 folded / 10 x 7 flat, 0.125 in bleed, 0.25 in safe | `05a_program_outside_10x7.png` (cover + back), `05b_program_inside_10x7.png` (order of day + signature sips) | 3075 x 2175 each @300 (= 10.25 x 7.25 in artwork; nothing within 0.2 in of the fold) | Cover: script title, names, date + venue caps, cluster garnish. Inside: all 8 program moments verbatim; both signature drinks with pet stories and dot-joined ingredients — echoing your drinks sign |
| 6 | Table signs, tables 1–6 — 5 x 7 in each, 300 DPI min | `06_table_sign_1.png` … `06_table_sign_6.png` | 1500 x 2100 each @300 | Identical layout: cluster garnish, TABLE letterspaced caps, Snell numeral (~880 px), sage rule; numerals taken from `seating_chart.json` tables 1–6 |
| — | Print-ready PDFs | `wedding_signage_suite.pdf` | 13 pages, one per piece, 300 dpi | Assembled locally with PIL (the Adobe connector exposes no image-to-PDF tool) |

## Copy fidelity

All wording — welcome wordings, menu, thank-you message, program, drinks, hashtag, all
120 guest names — was read programmatically from `wedding_copy.json` / `seating_chart.json`
and rendered verbatim; an automated pass asserts every string. Seven short connective
headers the layouts needed ("Welcome", "Dinner", "Find Your Seat", "Wedding Program",
"The Order of the Day", "Signature Sips", "TABLE") are noted as microcopy in the
trajectory; the hashtag and several copy lines are displayed in letterspaced caps as a
typographic treatment (characters unchanged).

## Honest limitations

- **Raster, not vector.** Deliverables are high-resolution PNG + a PNG-based PDF at the
  exact 300-DPI pixel targets. The brief's "preferably vector text" is not met: text is
  rasterized at print resolution. The editable source is `compose_5366.py`
  (parametric — re-runs reproduce every piece). PDF/X-4 prepress conversion remains a
  print-house step.
- **Floral elements are photographic** (processed from `floral_reference.jpg`), so sign
  fields are clean flat ivory while decor is photo-real — intentional, and nothing is
  upscaled from the low-res AI drafts (those were not used at all).
- Pieces 1, 2, 4 are exported at the spec's exact px targets (trim size); bleed for the
  large boards is a mounting/print-house matter as these are typically printed oversize
  and trimmed. Pieces 3 and 5 include the requested 0.125 in bleed in the artwork;
  piece 6 is at exact 5 x 7 trim per the px target in `print_spec.json`.
- The PLAN's export note said "(12 pages)" but lists 13 piece files; the PDF carries
  **13 pages** — one page per piece, which matches the deliverable list.

input_assets.what_it_is: The verbatim wording source for every piece: welcome wordings, 3-course menu, thank-you message, 8 program moments, both signature drinks and the hashtag.

items.question: Do the two signature-drink entries in wedding_copy.json match the client's untouchable sign exactly — 'The Barnaby' with Bourbon, Sweet Vermouth, Angostura Bitters, Cherry Garnish, and 'The Clementine' with Gin, Fresh Grapefruit Juice, Elderflower Liqueur, Sparkling Wine?
items.guidance: Open signature_drinks_sign.png and read its two ingredient lists, then compare word-for-word against signature_drinks[].name and ingredients in the JSON. Any added, dropped or reworded ingredient is a fail — the copy deck must be internally consistent with the anchor sign the new family coordinates with.
items.reference: Yes — both drink names and all eight ingredient strings are identical between the JSON and the painted sign.

items.question: How faithfully does the copy deck embody Maren's persona — 'warm, gracious, and quietly precise' — while locking every mandated fact (Saturday, September 19, 2026; Willowmere Estate; Cedar Hollow; ceremony 4:30 PM on the south lawn; #HappilyEverHartwell; 8 program moments running The Processional through Last Dance)?
items.guidance: 5 = every fact present and exactly spelled, tone reads like a gracious bride (e.g. 'Our hearts are overflowing with gratitude...'), program runs in believable chronological order processional-to-last-dance. 3 = facts correct but tone generic or program order odd. 1 = a hard fact is wrong or missing (wrong date, missing hashtag, fewer than 6 program moments).
items.reference: 5 — date/venue/town/times all present, hashtag exact, 8 ordered moments from 'The Processional' to 'Last Dance', and the thank-you and pet-story lines carry the warm-precise voice.

items.question: Transcribe the two menu dish names that carry accented characters, exactly as written in wedding_copy.json.
items.guidance: Look in menu.courses: First Course choice 2 and Dessert choice 1. Copy the names character-for-character including every accent — these are the strings the menu card must reproduce.
items.reference: 'Butternut Squash Velouté' and 'Classic Crème Brûlée'.

input_assets.what_it_is: The final 120-guest name list — 15 tables of 8, alphabetized by surname within each table — that the 47.2x35.4 in seating chart and the six table signs are built from.

items.question: How many tables and guests does seating_chart.json contain, and which two guests carry a 'Jr.' suffix (give name and table number)?
items.guidance: Count tables[] and the guests in each; scan for suffixes. The suffixed names are the canary strings for verbatim rendering downstream.
items.reference: 15 tables x 8 guests = 120. 'Martinez, Michael Jr.' at table 13 and 'Perez, Robert Jr.' at table 14.

items.question: How realistic and production-ready is the guest list itself — believable diverse names, married couples sharing surnames at the same table (Anderson, Baker, Chen at table 1; Quinn, Patel, Rodriguez at table 6), all 120 unique, and correctly alphabetized by surname within every table?
items.guidance: 5 = no placeholder or celebrity names, alphabetization holds in all 15 tables (e.g. table 1 runs Adams-Anderson-Anderson-Baker-Baker-Bennett-Chen-Chen), plausible cultural mix (Ito, Kim, Nguyen, Patel, Xiong, Washington). 3 = one or two ordering slips. 1 = duplicate names or 'Guest One'-style filler.
items.reference: 5 — list is clean, unique, alphabetized in every table, with natural couples and a believable mix; head_table_note correctly says Maren & Elliot sit at an unnumbered sweetheart table and tables 1-6 get printed signs.

items.question: Is the CSV render (seating_chart.csv) ambiguous as a standalone source — i.e., does it flatten each table's eight 'Surname, First' names into one comma-joined string where name boundaries can no longer be parsed reliably?
items.guidance: Open the CSV: row 1 reads table 1's guests as "Adams, Jennifer, Anderson, Laura, ..." — sixteen comma-separated tokens for eight people. Decide whether a designer working only from the CSV could mis-split names, making the JSON the only safe source.
items.reference: Yes — the CSV is ambiguous (commas serve both as name-internal separators and list separators); the JSON must be treated as canonical, which the execution did.

input_assets.what_it_is: The transcription of all six deliverables' trim sizes, pixel targets, DPI, bleed and safe margins — the contract the suite must be built to exactly.

items.question: Do the six px targets in print_spec.json match the client brief exactly: 6000x9000, 10620x7080, min 1200x2700 at final trim, 14160x10620, 3000x2100 flat, and 1500x2100 each?
items.guidance: Compare each deliverables[].px_target against the numbered list in TASK.md. The brief says 'Every number above is also transcribed in print_spec.json — please build to it exactly', so any transcription drift would poison every downstream export.
items.reference: Yes — all six pixel targets, trim sizes and 300 DPI figures match the brief's numbers exactly.

items.question: How usable is print_spec.json as an unambiguous build contract, given its internal tensions — bleed_in 0.125 and safe_margin_in 0.25 are stated for ALL six pieces (including the large boards the brief never required bleed on), the Wedding Program px_target is '3000 x 2100 px flat' with bleed listed separately, and the program's orientation is 'portrait folded' for a landscape 10x7 flat sheet?
items.guidance: 5 = a print designer could build all six pieces without asking a question. 3 = the bleed-vs-px-target arithmetic (does 3000x2100 include the 0.125 in bleed or not?) forces an assumption that must be disclosed. 1 = contradictory numbers that would produce a wrong-size file.
items.reference: 3-4 — numbers are faithful to the brief but the spec leaves bleed-inclusive vs trim-exact ambiguous for pieces 1, 2, 4, 6; the execution resolved it by exporting boards trim-exact and adding bleed only to pieces 3 and 5, and disclosed that choice in outputs/README.md.

input_assets.display: Client's signature drinks sign (the untouchable anchor)
input_assets.what_it_is: The client-made playful bar sign — watercolor pet portraits with 'The Barnaby' and 'The Clementine' — that must remain byte-identical while the new family coordinates with its palette and warmth.

items.question: Does this sign read as a finished, lovable client-made piece worth anchoring a family to — 'Signature Sips' in hand-script, a loose watercolor dog above 'The Barnaby' with its bourbon build, a watercolor cat above 'The Clementine' with its gin-spritz build, blush and sage washes on ivory, all lettering charcoal?
items.guidance: 5 = every word crisply legible, charming portraits, palette clearly ivory #FBF7EF / blush #EAC9C1 / sage #9CAF88 / charcoal #3B3B38, no stray text or mockup environment. 3 = legible but with soft/pixelated serif passages (the pipeline QC flagged slight pixelation in the ingredient lines). 1 = garbled text or off-palette.
items.reference: 4-5 — all wording exact and legible, flat front-on artwork, palette on-brief; minor softness in the small serif ingredient lines is the only blemish (QC vision score 9/10).

items.question: Do the two pet portraits actually match the persona's stated animals — Barnaby a GOLDEN RETRIEVER and Clementine an ORANGE TABBY — as painted?
items.guidance: Look at the pixels, not the labels: the dog has long feathered russet ears and a domed head that reads closer to a cocker spaniel than a golden retriever, and the cat is a grey-brown tabby with a white chest, not orange. Answer No if either animal visibly contradicts its stated breed/coloring.
items.reference: No — the dog is spaniel-like rather than clearly a golden retriever and the cat is not an orange tabby; a fair client-simulation flaw to note, though it binds the AI freelancer to nothing since the sign must stay untouched.

items.question: As the anchor the new family must coordinate with 'in palette and warmth', how strong and extractable is the sign's palette signal — ivory ground, loose blush and sage washes, charcoal lettering — for a designer who must echo it without copying its watercolor style?
items.guidance: Squint test: do the blush #EAC9C1 and sage #9CAF88 zones read as the two accent hues a coordinated family would pick up, with charcoal #3B3B38 as the obvious ink color? 5 = palette is unambiguous and the warmth is easy to translate into a more refined system. 3 = accents so muted (the QC noted they could be more vibrant) that a family built from them might drift. 1 = palette unreadable.
items.reference: 4 — the blush/sage/charcoal-on-ivory recipe is clearly extractable (and the delivered suite demonstrably extracted it); the washes are on the muted side, as the pipeline's own QC observed.

input_assets.display: Client's failed AI drafts (welcome sign + seating chart)
input_assets.what_it_is: The two AI-generated drafts the client wants rescued — they show the sign types needed while exhibiting exactly the low-res, generic, off-palette problems described in the brief.

items.question: Do these drafts convincingly motivate the job — waxy over-smooth AI florals, visible softness from low-resolution generation (both were degraded to ~479 px wide before upscaling), the welcome sign trapped inside a gold-frame easel mockup rather than flat artwork, and a seating chart filled with placeholder guests?
items.guidance: 5 = a print professional would immediately agree these cannot go to large format: mushy detail, airbrushed gradients, mockup environment baked in. 3 = generic but technically usable. 1 = the drafts look fine, undermining the brief's premise.
items.reference: 5 — the welcome sign reads 'Welcome / Maren & Elliot / Saturday, September 19, 2026' in gold script but is soft, framed and pastel-generic; the seating chart's small text is illegible mush. The premise holds.

items.question: List the defects you can spot in draft_seating_chart.png's table-number cards (numbering and guest names).
items.guidance: Read the 36 cards row by row. Note any non-sequential jumps, duplicated numbers, and placeholder/garbled guest names. This is the strongest evidence the drafts are unusable.
items.reference: Numbers run 1-12 then jump (13, 20, 23-26, 27-30, 35-36, 37-42) and DUPLICATE: 41 and 42 each appear twice in the bottom rows (last row reads 41, 42, 43, 44, 45, 48); guest names are placeholders like 'Guest One', 'Guest Two', 'Guest Ten' and lorem-style gibberish; 36 cards shown though the real wedding has 15 tables.

items.question: Do the drafts violate the client's stated palette — i.e., do they contain prominent pastel BLUE and lilac florals that appear nowhere in the ivory #FBF7EF / blush #EAC9C1 / sage #9CAF88 / charcoal #3B3B38 scheme?
items.guidance: Check the watercolor flowers on both drafts for blue and purple blooms, and the gold/tan script ink versus the mandated charcoal lettering.
items.reference: Yes — both drafts feature pastel blue and lilac flowers and the welcome sign's lettering is gold, confirming they 'do not coordinate' with the anchor sign's palette.

input_assets.what_it_is: The documentary-style photo of a ceremony-arch arrangement — blush garden roses, cream ranunculus, sage eucalyptus, white delphinium on a wooden arch — that seeds every floral element in the soft-floral direction.

items.question: How believable is this as a real florist's photo — asymmetric crescent of blush roses, cream ranunculus, eucalyptus and white delphinium climbing a simple wooden arch, estate manor softly blurred behind, two or three fallen petals on the lawn, natural daylight with gentle grain?
items.guidance: 5 = passes as an actual 35mm-style documentary shot: believable foliage overlap, uneven stem spacing, realistic depth of field, no CGI gloss or text/watermark anywhere. 3 = pretty but with telltale AI tells (impossible stems, repeated blooms). 1 = obviously synthetic or contains lettering.
items.reference: 5 — the named flowers are all identifiable, the manor and lawn bokeh is consistent, fallen petals sit lower-right, and there is no text or watermark (pipeline QC scored it 10/10).

items.question: Is the arrangement practically reusable for the Adobe cutout workflow — a dense, well-lit bloom mass separable from the plain lawn background — while noting that the bare wooden arch post runs through it and would need cropping out of any cutout?
items.guidance: Judge edge separability (flowers vs grass), whether the densest rose cluster could be smart-cropped square as a garnish, and whether the dark wooden stand would contaminate a remove-background result if not trimmed.
items.reference: Yes — cleanly separable and crop-friendly; the visible wooden stand is the one hazard, and the execution's verify step records cropping it ('arch stand crop 0.82h') after spotting it in review.

input_assets.what_it_is: The client's reference for creative direction Option 2: an ultra-minimal welcome sign for a different couple in fine near-black calligraphy on ivory.

items.question: Does this reference cleanly communicate the 'elevated black script' option — exactly three centered lines ('WELCOME TO THE WEDDING OF' in letterspaced serif caps, 'Olivia & James' in large flowing calligraphy, 'June 20, 2026' in slender script), generous negative space, zero florals or ornament?
items.guidance: 5 = confident thick-and-thin calligraphic strokes, perfectly legible, nothing on the board but the three lines — a designer could infer the entire direction from it. 3 = right idea but extra ornament or wobbly letterforms. 1 = garbled text or florals present.
items.reference: 5 — exactly the three specified lines for placeholder couple Olivia & James, crisp calligraphy, no ornament whatsoever.

items.question: Is it unambiguous that this sign is a STYLE reference rather than content to reproduce — i.e., it names a different couple ('Olivia & James', June 20, 2026) and none of its wording belongs on the Hartwell pieces?
items.guidance: Confirm the names and date differ from Maren & Elliot Hartwell / September 19, 2026, so any leakage of 'Olivia & James' into the deliverables would be a contamination error.
items.reference: Yes — different couple and date; the delivered suite correctly contains no trace of Olivia, James, or June 20.

outputs.asked: Welcome Gathering Sign, 20 x 30 in, minimum 6000 x 9000 px at 300 DPI.

items.question: Is the file exactly 6000 x 9000 px (20 x 30 in at 300 DPI), with no sign of upscaling from the low-res AI draft?
items.guidance: Check pixel dimensions, then zoom to the script edges and floral photo detail: edges should be render-crisp, florals photographic from floral_reference.jpg — not the waxy pastel blooms of draft_welcome_sign.png.
items.reference: Yes — 6000 x 9000 confirmed (verify_report.json dims_ok), florals are the photographic arch cutout, draft was not reused.

items.question: Transcribe every line of text on the sign top-to-bottom and note any character that deviates from welcome_wordings.gathering in wedding_copy.json.

task_level.question: Does the six-piece suite read as ONE coordinated family — shared ivory fields, the same arch-cutout corner pattern and rose-cluster garnish recurring across pieces, Snell script + Hoefler serif everywhere, sage hairlines — while the untouched signature drinks sign still sits comfortably beside it as 'the playful, personal piece'?

task_level.question: Were the two hard constraints honored: (a) signature_drinks_sign.png never altered (README records md5 542032a80cf91381032a952126846c41, byte-identical), and (b) the low-res AI drafts never reused or upscaled — every floral element instead derived from floral_reference.jpg via connector operations (auto-tone, remove-background, smart crop, generative expand)?

task_level.question: Acting as Maren — a bride who is 'quietly precise', burned once by AI mush, and explicitly afraid of large-format printing risk — would you accept this suite and release payment?
task_level.guidance: Weigh: zero copy errors across roughly 180 rendered strings including 120 names; exact resolutions; coordinated look; honest README. Against: raster-not-vector PDF, no native Adobe source files, no bleed/marks on the large boards, the lawn-green garnish square, and the top-heavy 20x30 welcome sign. 5 = pay in full, hang the signs. 4 = pay after small revisions (e.g. mask the garnish). 3 = request one revision round before paying. 2 = significant rework. 1 = reject.

Then confirm PLAN.md states the direction was chosen 'per recorded decision'.
task_level.reference: Yes — all six pieces are coherently soft-floral and PLAN.md records the Option 1 decision; a both-directions pitch was permitted but not required.

task_level.question: Name the single biggest weakness of this delivery end-to-end, and the first revision you would request as the client.
task_level.guidance: Consider the disclosed gaps (raster text in the PDF, no native source files, bleed only on pieces 3 and 5) and the visual nits (lawn visible in the rose-cluster garnish on table signs/menu back/program cover, top-heavy lower half on the 20x30 sign, dainty old-style 'TABLE' numerals on the seating chart panels). Pick the one that most threatens client acceptance and justify briefly.

tasks.title: graphic designer / print designer to recreate a two-sided DIN A5 package
tasks.task_type: Blausweta DIN A5 Package Insert — rebuild 2-sided print
tasks.one_line_task: Rebuild a German shaving retailer's two-sided DIN A5 package insert from rough AI drafts into a clean print-ready layout that moves eBay buyers to the official online shop.
tasks.one_line_outputs: A print-ready two-sided A5 file (148×210 mm + 2 mm bleed) with separate hi-res front/back previews and the official logo as vector.
tasks.brief: We need a professional graphic designer / print designer to recreate a two-sided DIN A5 package insert for Blausweta-Rasur. The current visual references are included in the asset package. Please do not simply upscale the AI drafts. The task is to rebuild the front and back side cleanly in Illustrator/InDesign as a proper print-ready layout with editable text, real vector logo, clean icons, correct bleed, and final PDF export for SAXOPRINT. Blausweta-Rasur is a German specialist retailer / online shop for shaving, grooming, razors, razor blades, care products and barber supplies. The shop serves private customers, barbers, salons and resellers. Blausweta-Rasur is already established on eBay and now wants to motivate marketplace customers to order directly in the official online shop. The insert should communicate: professional specialist retailer, reliable shipping, fair prices, direct shop advantages, loyalty/bonus points, referral benefits and shop-exclusive discount codes. Main goal: Customers who may know Blausweta from eBay or another marketplace should switch to the official online shop. Important campaign message: - Customers can save more directly in the Blausweta online shop. - First direct shop order after switching from eBay or another marketplace: 7% discount with code SHOP7. - Follow-up order / next direct shop order: 5% discount with code DANKE5. - Bonus points are collected additionally and can be redeemed. - Referral discounts are also available. Important: All discounts are percentages. Do not use € discount wording. Required deliverables: 1. Editable source file: Adobe Illustrator .ai preferred, or InDesign .indd with linked assets. 2. Print-ready two-sided PDF for SAXOPRINT, CMYK, PDF/X-4 preferred. 3. Front side and back side as separate high-resolution PNG/JPG previews. 4. All text must remain editable in the source file. 5. Use the official logo from the provided logo files. Do not trace/recreate the logo from the AI draft. 6. Use the clean warehouse header image without any baked-in logo. The logo will be positioned manually in the layout. Format / print setup: - DIN A5 flyer / package insert, double-sided, portrait orientation. - Final trim size: 148 × 210 mm. - Artwork size with bleed: 152 × 214 mm. - Bleed: 2 mm on every side. - Keep all important text/logos/icons at least 3 mm inside the final trim edge. Prefer 5 mm for safety. - Background images that touch the edge must extend into the bleed. - CMYK color mode. - Minimum image resolution: 250 dpi at final size, ideally 300 dpi. - Embed all fonts or convert to outlines in the print PDF. Design direction: Keep the style very close to the references: - Clean, modern, professional specialist retailer look. - White background with deep navy / purple accents. - No colorful mix. Stay in the Blausweta navy/purple world. - No gold, no green, no Trustpilot badge. - Avoid wedding/thank-you-card feeling. - Avoid playful hearts and handwritten romantic scripts. - The back side should feel like a professional shop benefit page. - The front side can remain warmer and personal, but still businesslike. Coupon details: SHOP7 = 7% welcome/switch discount for first direct shop order after switching from eBay or another platform. One-time use only. DANKE5 = 5% discount for the next direct shop order / follow-up order.
tasks.mcp_workflow: asset_add_file (logo files + clean warehouse header image + references) -> image_vectorize (icons if needed) -> search_design (Express A5 flyer template) -> fill_text (shop-benefit copy + codes SHOP7 / DANKE5) -> image_crop_and_resize (152x214mm w/ 2mm bleed) -> document_convert_pdf (CMYK PDF/X-4) -> asset_preview_file

Front stage 4: two code chips built ONLY from codes[].code + discount_pct (SHOP7 navy, DANKE5 purple) plus the cta_de caption; per plan the front stays inviting and carries no legal text.
Back stage 1: full-bleed navy header band with the composed German microcopy section header (Ihre Vorteile im offiziellen Online-Shop) — 1 of the 4 declared microcopy strings this task adds.

Back stage 2: six back_benefits rows in a 2x3 grid, icon cutouts matched by icon_hint (truck/percent/star/people); shield + package have no supplied icon so those two rows use navy numbered circles (4, 5) as the documented fallback; titles wrap at one fixed size for consistency.

Back stage 3: two navy-outline coupon boxes — code bold + percent purple + applies_to_de wrapped, purple einmalig chip (microcopy) only where one_time is true — then loyalty_de and referral_de lines with their inline icons.

Back stage 4: footer placed by measured flow (no fixed Y) — purple hairline, cta_de repeated bold-centered, legal_footer_de in light 30px on a 900px measure so the two lines break balanced; end Y asserted inside the 5mm safe zone (2419 <= 2445).
Front exported at exact artwork size 1795x2528 px (152x214mm @300dpi = 148x210 trim + 2mm bleed) — the hi-res front preview deliverable.
Back exported at exact artwork size 1795x2528 px @300dpi — the hi-res back preview deliverable.

Two-page print PDF on a 1895x2628 slug with crop marks at the 148x210mm trim box — assembled LOCALLY with PIL because the connector has no image-to-PDF tool; CMYK/PDF-X-4 prepress conversion is a print-house step (stated in README).

Visual read of the first composed back found the loyalty/referral lines colliding with the fixed-Y footer (lines ended y=2503 vs hairline 2255) and the legal text overflowing the safe zone (2469 > 2445); back rebuilt with a measured vertical budget, titles wrapping instead of shrinking, and a flow-placed footer — re-read clean.
verify_3437.py asserts front/back exactly 1795x2528 @300dpi, the PDF has 2 pages at the 454.80x630.72pt slug (1895x2628 px @300dpi), and the vector logo SVG is real — all checks passed.

Audit of all 34 rendered strings: every one is a verbatim _de value, a code/percent derived from codes[], or one of the 4 declared microcopy strings; all 21 _de fields appear in the layout, zero _en strings leaked and no euro symbol anywhere (percent-only rule).

Final visual read of both sides: umlauts and sharp-s render correctly in Helvetica Neue, codes read exactly SHOP7 and DANKE5 with 7 % / 5 %, palette is strictly navy/purple/white (no gold or green design elements), no clipped or colliding text on either side.

tasks.readme:
# Task 3437 — Blausweta-Rasur two-sided DIN A5 package insert: deliverables

Print language: German (all copy rendered verbatim from `input_assets/insert_copy.json`,
`_de` fields only). Palette strictly navy `#1E2D5C` / purple `#5B4A9E` / white. Type:
Helvetica Neue bold / medium / regular / light.

## Asked → produced

| # | Asked (TASK.md) | Produced file(s) | Exact px / dpi | How it was made |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Print-ready two-sided PDF for SAXOPRINT — DIN A5 portrait, trim 148×210 mm, artwork 152×214 mm (2 mm bleed), CMYK, PDF/X-4, fonts embedded/outlined | `insert_print_A5.pdf` | 2 pages, 1895×2628 px slug @300 dpi (= 454.80×630.72 pt) with crop marks at the 148×210 mm trim box; artwork 1795×2528 px incl. 2 mm bleed | Front + back artwork composed locally (PIL/compose_lib) from connector-processed elements, then assembled locally into the PDF because the Adobe connector has no image→PDF tool. **Honest limitation:** the file is RGB raster with text rendered (not live fonts); CMYK conversion and PDF/X-4 prepress packaging are a print-house step — SAXOPRINT's preflight performs this conversion, but a strict PDF/X-4 source requires the human .ai handoff below. |
| 2 | Front + back as separate high-resolution previews | `front_152x214mm.png`, `back_152x214mm.png` | 1795×2528 px each @300 dpi (152×214 mm artwork = 148×210 trim + 2 mm bleed) | Full-resolution composition: connector ops (auto-tone → cooler color temperature → 2:1 reframe on the warehouse photo; background removal on all four icons; logo cutout + vectorize) + local layout per PLAN. All content kept ≥5 mm inside trim (verified: front content ends y=2326, back y=2419, limit 2445). |
| 3 | Editable source (.ai preferred, or .indd with linked assets, text editable) | Not producible by this pipeline — see note | — | **Honest limitation (per the brief's own feasibility note):** the connector exposes no Illustrator/InDesign authoring API, so no real `.ai`/`.indd` can be generated here; that is a human handoff step. Provided to make that handoff trivial: `compose_3437.py` (fully parametric layout program, every string data-bound to `insert_copy.json`), `support/logo_blausweta.svg` (true vector), and all processed elements in `work/`. |
| 4 | Safe zones ≥3 mm (5 mm preferred), edge images extended into bleed, images ≥250 dpi (ideally 300 dpi) | properties of all files above | content ≥5 mm inside trim (≥83 px from artwork edge); back navy header band runs full-bleed; photo band is inset by design and does not touch trim | Layout is flow-measured with asserts; the warehouse photo band is used at ~300 dpi effective (source band 1536×768 cover-fitted to 1495×760 px at 0.99× scale — no upscaling anywhere). |

Supporting file: `support/logo_blausweta.svg` — real vector logo produced by the
connector's `image_vectorize` (Illustrator API), for the print-source handoff.

## Acceptance-criteria check

- Trim 148×210 mm / artwork 152×214 mm with 2 mm bleed: exact (1795×2528 px @300 dpi); 5 mm safe zone respected and asserted programmatically.
- CMYK / PDF/X-4: **not fully met by this pipeline** — RGB output, conversion is a print-house / human-.ai step (stated above).
- Official logo placed as-is: yes — the original `logo_blausweta.png` on the white front band (never traced); the warehouse header is the clean supplied photo, logo positioned separately.
- Codes exactly SHOP7 (7 %, first direct order, marked `einmalig`) and DANKE5 (5 %, follow-up order); percentages only — audited: no `€` glyph anywhere, all strings data-bound.
- Navy/purple on white only, no gold/green/Trustpilot, nothing wedding-y; back reads as a professional benefits page (navy band + 2×3 benefit grid + coupon boxes + legal footer).
- Separate hi-res previews: yes. Editable .ai/.indd: human handoff (see row 3).

## Notes on element provenance

- Warehouse photo: `image_apply_auto_tone` → `image_adjust_color_temperature` (slightly cooler, businesslike) → `image_crop_and_resize` to 2:1 (Adobe connector, requestIds in `trajectory.json`).
- Icons (truck/percent/star/people): `image_remove_background` cutouts (connector). The `shield` and `package` benefit rows have **no supplied icon** — rendered as navy numbered circles (4, 5) as the documented fallback.
- Logo: connector cutout retained as a supporting asset, but the front places the **original** logo file on white for placement fidelity (the cutout dropped the white interior letterforms); `image_vectorize` produced the SVG.
- Microcopy added (4 strings, logged in the trajectory): the back band header "Ihre Vorteile im offiziellen Online-Shop", the "einmalig" badge, and the fallback row numerals "4"/"5". Everything else is verbatim client copy.

input_assets.what_it_is: The complete German copy deck for the insert: front headline/subline/intro, six back_benefits rows with icon_hints, both discount codes with conditions, loyalty/referral lines, CTA and legal footer.

items.question: Do the two code objects specify exactly SHOP7 with discount_pct 7 and one_time true, and DANKE5 with discount_pct 5 and one_time false — and is the Euro sign (€) absent from every German string in the file (percentages-only rule)?
items.guidance: Open insert_copy.json and read the codes array. SHOP7's applies_to_de must scope it to the FIRST direct shop order ('Für Ihre erste direkte Shop-Bestellung als Neukunde.') and DANKE5 to follow-up orders ('Für jede weitere Shop-Bestellung als unser geschätzter Kunde.'). Then search the whole file for '€' or any Euro-amount discount wording — the brief forbids it everywhere.
items.reference: Yes — codes are exactly SHOP7/7/one_time:true and DANKE5/5/one_time:false with the correct first-order vs follow-up scoping, and no € sign or Euro-amount wording appears anywhere; the legal footer even states 'Alle Rabatte sind in Prozent.'

items.question: Rate the German copy quality: is every _de field natural, native-quality German in the formal Sie-form with the warm-but-businesslike specialist-retailer voice the persona demands?
items.guidance: Read all 21 _de fields. 5 = flawless idiomatic German, consistently Sie-form (e.g. 'Wir danken Ihnen herzlich für Ihr Vertrauen...', 'Profitieren Sie von...', 'Vertrauen Sie auf unser Expertenwissen...'), titles ≤5 words, descriptions concrete and ≤~18 words. 3 = grammatical but stiff/translated-sounding or one slip in register. 1 = grammar errors, machine-translation artifacts, or informal Du-form (note the old draft_back used 'WIR SIND FÜR DICH DA' — that register must NOT appear here). Minor flag: the truck row repeats 'Schneller, zuverlässiger Versand' verbatim as both title and the start of its description — judge whether that redundancy costs polish.
items.reference: A 5 reads like a German shop owner wrote it: correct umlauts/ß (regelmäßigen, größte, geschätzter), consistent Sie-form, percentages only. The supplied copy is near-native; the title/desc repetition in the Versand row is the one visible blemish, so 4-5 is the expected score.

items.question: Transcribe the cta_de string exactly as it appears in insert_copy.json, and state the shop domain it names.
items.guidance: Copy the value character-for-character including punctuation. This exact line must later appear on BOTH sides of the printed insert, so the transcription is the ground truth for the output checks.
items.reference: "Besuchen Sie uns jetzt: www.blausweta-rasur.de" — domain www.blausweta-rasur.de, a plausible official .de domain matching the brand name.

input_assets.what_it_is: The official Blausweta-Rasur logo file the brief orders to be placed as-is (never traced or rebuilt from the AI drafts): a navy wordmark with a navy/purple straight-razor motif on white, 1024×1024.

items.question: Does the wordmark read EXACTLY 'Blausweta-Rasur' — capital B, capital R, with the hyphen, no garbled or doubled letters?
items.guidance: AI-generated logos frequently garble letterforms. Zoom in and read every glyph: B-l-a-u-s-w-e-t-a, hyphen, R-a-s-u-r. Compare against the brand name in persona/INTAKE ('Blausweta-Rasur').
items.reference: Yes — the wordmark is cleanly set as 'Blausweta-Rasur' in a navy bold sans-serif with the hyphen present and no spelling defects.

items.question: Rate the logo's production quality as a brand mark: flat vector-style edges, strictly deep-navy #1E2D5C + rich-purple #5B4A9E on plain white, one minimal straight-razor motif, no gradients/shadows/tagline.
items.guidance: 5 = crisp anti-aliased but vector-clean edges on both the type and the open straight razor (purple handle with two white pivot dots, navy blade), exactly the two brand colors on white, nothing else. 3 = correct concept but soft edges, off-palette tints, or a slightly mushy razor silhouette. 1 = gradients, drop shadows, extra text, or a second motif.
items.reference: A 5: the supplied mark is a clean flat wordmark+razor lockup — purple razor handle with white pivot dots, navy blade and navy type — with no gradients, no tagline, and edges crisp enough to print small on an A5 insert.

items.question: Rate the file's usability for the layout workflow: clearspace, croppability and legibility at print size (the front places it about 64 mm wide at the top of an A5 page).
items.guidance: The 1024×1024 canvas has the lockup occupying roughly the middle horizontal band with generous white margins — good for a tight optical crop. Check: would a background-removal cutout endanger the white interior counters of the letters (a known hazard the trajectory hit)? 5 = ample clearspace, scales down legibly, white-on-white counters are the only cutout risk. 3 = cramped margins or thin strokes that fuzz at small sizes. 1 = clipped edges or illegible when small.
items.reference: A 5: generous clearspace on all sides, the wordmark stays legible scaled to ~64 mm wide, and the only processing hazard is that naive background removal drops the white letter counters — which is why placing the original on white (as the brief allows) is the safe play.

input_assets.display: Client AI drafts (front + back) — the bad references
input_assets.what_it_is: The client's own two AI drafts the rebuild must replace: a wedding-card-style front and a clumsy benefits-list back. They define the structure to keep and the styling to avoid; the brief says do NOT upscale them.

items.question: Rate draft_front.png as a believable 'bad first AI draft': does it exhibit precisely the flaws the brief bans — handwritten romantic script ('Vielen Dank'), ornamental corner filigree, watercolor pastel washes, small hearts — while still staying in the navy/purple world?
items.guidance: The draft should justify the rebuild. Look for: rough 'BLAUSWETA-RASUR' wordmark band at top, script headline, flourish dividers, at least two heart motifs, patchy lavender watercolor blotches, the lines 'Wir danken Ihnen für Ihre Bestellung' and 'Genießen Sie Ihre Rasur', photographed-on-wood-table presentation. 5 = unmistakably wedding-thank-you-card styling in navy/purple, exactly what the client said looks wrong. 3 = some flagged flaws missing. 1 = it accidentally looks professional (no contrast with the rebuild).
items.reference: A 5: the draft is a soft pastel thank-you card — script 'Vielen Dank', filigree corners, hearts, watercolor — that obviously violates the no-wedding-feel rule while keeping the navy/purple palette, making the clean rebuild's purpose self-evident.

items.question: Does draft_back.png supply the structural skeleton the rebuild keeps (icon benefit rows + a boxed coupon area at the bottom) while containing styling violations the brief explicitly bans — notably the GOLD/yellow thumbs-up and gold gift-box clip-art icons and the informal Du-form copy ('WIR SIND FÜR DICH DA', 'BEI DEINER NÄCHSTEN BESTELLUNG')?
items.guidance: Confirm both halves: (a) structure — five bullet rows ('GROSSE AUSWAHL', 'SICHERE BESTELLUNG', 'KUNDEN-SERVICE', 'ZUFRIEDENHEITS GARANTIE', 'EXKLUSIVE ANGEBOTE') each with a clip-art icon, plus the 'DEIN GUTSCHEIN-CODE!' coupon box; (b) violations — mismatched icon styles (navy gear, purple smiley shopping cart, yellow thumbs-up, gold gift box), gold tones breaking the no-gold rule, and Du-form German breaking the Sie-form voice.
items.reference: Yes — the back draft is a benefits list + coupon box in structure, but its gold/yellow icons, mismatched clip-art, uneven spacing and Du-form copy are exactly the off-brief elements the rebuild must eliminate.

items.question: Transcribe the coupon code and discount advertised in draft_back.png's coupon box — this legacy offer must appear NOWHERE in the rebuilt insert.
items.guidance: Read the purple coupon box near the bottom of draft_back.png and the small-print line under it. Record the code, the percentage, and the expiry wording. The rebuild replaces this offer with SHOP7/DANKE5, so any leak of this string into the outputs is a hard failure.
items.reference: Code 'RASUR-FREUNDE10' for '10% RABATT BEI DEINER NÄCHSTEN BESTELLUNG', small print 'Gültig bis zum 31.12.2024. Nur ein Code pro Person. *Es gelten die AGB.' — none of which may survive into the rebuilt front/back.

input_assets.what_it_is: The supplied clean packing-area photo (1536×1024) for the front: a small e-commerce warehouse with wooden packing bench, kraft cartons, tissue paper and tape dispenser — delivered WITHOUT any baked-in logo so the logo can be positioned separately in layout.

items.question: Is the photo completely free of text, letters, numbers, watermarks and logos — on the shelf cartons, the white product boxes, the navy jars/bottles, the tape dispenser and everywhere else?
items.guidance: The brief's whole point is a 'clean warehouse header image (no baked-in logo)'. Scan every box face and label-sized surface at zoom: the kraft shipping cartons on the bench, the stacked white cartons and navy product packaging on the metal shelving, the cosmetic bottles, the window area. Any legible or pseudo-legible glyph fails.
items.reference: Yes — all cartons and products are unbranded blanks; there is no text, watermark or logo anywhere in the frame.

items.question: Rate the photographic realism: does it pass as a candid documentary photo of a German specialist retailer's packing area rather than CGI or staged stock?
items.guidance: Look for the briefed lived-in cues: bright neutral daylight from the large left window, white-brick wall, metal shelving with plain boxes, wooden bench with kraft cartons + white tissue paper + tape dispenser, flattened cardboard stacked under the table, scuffed concrete floor, trailing green plant at right, shallow depth of field. 5 = believable working space with natural light falloff, plausible shadows and imperfections. 3 = mostly believable but with telltale AI smoothness, repeated box geometry or physics oddities (check shelf brackets and box edges). 1 = obviously synthetic gloss.
items.reference: A 5: convincing available-light documentary look — coherent window light, believable clutter (open carton with tissue, tape dispenser mid-bench), realistic materials; only forensic-level inspection of repeated cartons would hint it is generated.

items.question: Rate its usability for this layout: calm space along the top edge (composed so a logo could sit there), croppable to the wide 2:1 band the front uses with the packing bench prominent, and enough resolution for ~300 dpi at final size.
items.guidance: The plan reframes it to 2:1 centred on the bench/box ('cardboard packing box on bench' focus). Check: the upper third is a calm white wall suitable as headroom; a 2:1 centre crop keeps bench, open kraft box and tissue paper as the subject; 1536 px width cover-fitted to a 1495×760 px band means ~1.0× scale, i.e. ~300 dpi effective with no upscaling. 5 = all three hold. 3 = crop forces awkward amputation of the bench or marginal resolution. 1 = busy top edge or sub-250-dpi softness.
items.reference: A 5: the photo was composed with calmer wall space up top, the 2:1 reframe keeps the bench and open box dominant, and at 1495×760 px placement the source supplies ~300 dpi effective resolution without upscaling.

input_assets.display: Benefit icon set (truck / percent / star / people)
input_assets.what_it_is: Four flat 1024×1024 icons for the back-page benefit rows — delivery truck (shipping), percent-in-circle badge (prices), star with three dots (bonus points), two people with arrow (referral) — in thick navy strokes with purple accents on white.

items.question: Rate the set's stylistic consistency: do all four icons read as one family — thick rounded deep-navy #1E2D5C strokes with rich-purple #5B4A9E accents on pure white, similar visual weight, padding and minimalism?
items.guidance: Compare stroke weight and corner rounding across truck, percent badge, star and people. Known blemishes from QC to weigh: the stray purple dash on the truck's front bumper, slightly thinner purple motion dashes behind the truck, the referral arrow being a purple FILL while the figures are stroked+filled, and the loyalty star's three floating purple dots clustering to one side rather than arcing. 5 = reads as one commissioned set despite micro-flaws. 3 = noticeably divergent weights or fill-vs-stroke treatments (e.g. the referral figures are heavily filled while the percent badge is pure outline). 1 = looks like icons from four different libraries — the failure mode of draft_back.
items.reference: A 4-5: consistent navy/purple two-color system and rounded strokes; honest deductions for the truck's orphan purple dash, the solid-fill people vs outline-only star/percent, and the loyalty dots' ambiguous placement.

items.question: Is the % glyph inside icon_discount.png the ONLY glyph anywhere in the set — no letters, numbers, words or captions baked into any of the four icons?
items.guidance: The first generation attempt failed QC for text under each icon. Inspect all four at zoom, including the box symbol on the truck's cargo door (must be a pictogram, not a character) and the area below each icon.
items.reference: Yes — the percent sign in the discount badge is the sole glyph; the other three icons are purely pictographic with no baked-in text.

items.question: insert_copy.json assigns six icon_hints (percent, star, people, shield, package, truck). Which two hinted subjects have NO matching icon file in this supplied set, and which benefit rows does that leave icon-less?
items.guidance: Cross-reference the four files against the six back_benefits icon_hint values. This coverage gap is a real input deficiency the layout must visibly work around, so name the orphaned rows by their German titles.
items.reference: shield and package are missing — leaving 'Kompetente Fachberatung' (shield) and 'Umfassendes Sortiment' (package) without supplied icons; the execution substitutes navy numbered circles '4' and '5' for those rows.

outputs.asked: Deliverable 2 (front half): separate high-resolution front preview at full artwork size — 1795×2528 px = 152×214 mm @300 dpi (148×210 trim + 2 mm bleed).

items.question: Is every front string verbatim from insert_copy.json with correct German typography: headline 'Vielen Dank für Ihre Bestellung!', subline 'Willkommen in der Welt der traditionellen Rasur.', and the full two-sentence intro ('Wir danken Ihnen herzlich für Ihr Vertrauen und Ihren Einkauf bei uns. Entdecken Sie jetzt die volle Produktvielfalt und exklusive Vorteile in unserem offiziellen Online-Shop.') — umlauts (für ×3) rendered cleanly, no _en gloss leaked?
items.guidance: Compare character-for-character against insert_copy.json's front object. Check the ü glyphs at zoom for substitution boxes or wrong diacritics, confirm the exclamation mark on the headline, and confirm no English ('Thank you for your order!') appears anywhere.
items.reference: Yes — all three front fields render verbatim with clean umlauts; no English gloss and no paraphrasing.

items.question: Rate the front's layout craft: logo placed as-is on the white top band above a purple hairline, centered type stack with clear hierarchy (navy bold headline > purple medium subline > navy regular 3-line intro), rounded-corner photo band with navy keyline inset from the edges, then chips + CTA — all aligned, nothing colliding or clipped.
items.guidance: 5 = confident vertical rhythm: consistent center axis, even spacing between the six stacked elements, the warehouse band's 24 px rounded corners and thin navy keyline crisp, all content visibly inside the 5 mm safe zone (the README asserts front content ends y=2326 vs limit 2445). 3 = workable but with uneven gaps (e.g. dead space below the CTA), or the headline crowding the hairline. 1 = misalignment, overlaps or clipped descenders.
items.reference: A 5 looks like a designer's clean thank-you page: logo band, hairline, type stack, photo band, two chips, CTA — one centered axis, generous margins, no collisions. Note the bottom region under the CTA is relatively empty; judge whether that reads as airy or unfinished.

items.question: Rate brand/style compliance of the front: strictly navy #1E2D5C / purple #5B4A9E on white, warmer-and-personal yet businesslike, with zero residue of draft_front's wedding styling (no hearts, no filigree, no script type, no watercolor), and the warehouse photo graded cooler/neutral.
items.guidance: Sample the headline (should be the deep navy), subline and hairline (purple), chip fills (one navy, one purple). Confirm the typeface is a clean modern sans (Helvetica Neue) everywhere — no script. Compare the photo band's cast against the original warehouse_header.jpg: the connector applied auto-tone then a cooler temperature shift (a=6, b=-18), so the band should look slightly cooler/more neutral than the warm source. 5 = pure three-color world, warm tone carried by copy and photo content only. 3 = minor off-palette tints or a still-warm cast. 1 = any heart/flourish/script or extra colors beyond the photo's natural content.
items.reference: A 5: navy/purple/white discipline throughout, sans-serif only, and a visibly cooler, businesslike photo grade versus the source — warm in voice, not in styling.

items.question: Are the two code chips built ONLY from codes[] data — navy chip reading 'SHOP7  7 %' and purple chip 'DANKE5  5 %' — with no per-code conditions, no legal text and no € sign anywhere on the front?
items.guidance: The plan deliberately keeps conditions ('Für Ihre erste...' etc.), the 'einmalig' marker and legal_footer_de OFF the front to keep it inviting. Verify chip texts character-exact (SHOP7 not SHOP-7; the space-separated '7 %' per German convention), the CTA line 'Besuchen Sie uns jetzt: www.blausweta-rasur.de' beneath them, and scan the whole page for €.
items.reference: Yes — exactly two chips (SHOP7 7 % on navy, DANKE5 5 % on purple) plus the CTA; conditions and small print appear only on the back, and no € exists on the page.

outputs.asked: Deliverable 2 (back half): separate high-resolution back preview at full artwork size — the professional shop-benefits page with 2×3 grid, coupon boxes, loyalty/referral lines and legal footer.

items.question: Are all six benefit rows present with verbatim titles — 'Attraktive Shop-Preise', 'Exklusive Bonusprogramme', 'Freunde werben Freunde', 'Kompetente Fachberatung', 'Umfassendes Sortiment', 'Schneller, zuverlässiger Versand' — and is each supplied icon on its hinted row (percent→Shop-Preise, star→Bonusprogramme, people→Freunde, truck→Versand)?
items.guidance: Read the 2×3 grid row-major against insert_copy.json's back_benefits order (1 percent, 2 star, 3 people, 4 shield, 5 package, 6 truck). Confirm each desc_de paragraph is verbatim too (e.g. 'Sammeln Sie bei jedem Einkauf wertvolle Punkte...' under Bonusprogramme; '...Ihre Bestellung ist im Handumdrehen bei Ihnen.' under Versand). Any swapped icon (e.g. star next to Shop-Preise) fails.
items.reference: Yes — six rows in JSON order, titles and descriptions verbatim, with percent/star/people/truck icons each on the row their icon_hint dictates.

items.question: The shield and package rows had no supplied icons; the execution renders navy numbered circles '4' and '5' next to 'Kompetente Fachberatung' and 'Umfassendes Sortiment' as a documented fallback. Rate how acceptable this fallback is in the finished design.
items.guidance: This is a disclosed limitation (README + trajectory log it as microcopy/fallback). Judge it on craft and honesty, not just existence: do the circles match the icon column's size and stroke weight? Do they read as deliberate list numbering or as obvious placeholders? Does mixing pictogram rows (truck, star...) with numeral rows (4, 5) break the grid's professionalism — the exact mismatched-icons flaw draft_back was criticized for? 5 = visually consistent, a client might accept it as a style choice. 3 = noticeable inconsistency but tidy and honestly disclosed. 1 = looks like an error or undisclosed.
items.reference: Expected ~3: the circles are clean, navy, weight-matched and disclosed, but '4' and '5' floating among four pictograms (and numbering only rows 4-5 of an unnumbered list) reads as a gap a human finisher should replace with real shield/package icons.

items.question: Rate the coupon section's accuracy and craft: navy-outlined SHOP7 box with bold 'SHOP7', purple '7 %', verbatim condition 'Für Ihre erste direkte Shop-Bestellung als Neukunde.' and a purple 'einmalig' chip; DANKE5 box with '5 %', 'Für jede weitere Shop-Bestellung als unser geschätzter Kunde.' and NO einmalig chip; followed by the loyalty and referral lines with inline star/people icons.
items.guidance: Check one_time fidelity: the einmalig badge must appear in the SHOP7 box only (one_time:true) and be absent from DANKE5 (one_time:false). Verify the loyalty line 'Sammeln Sie bei jeder Shop-Bestellung Bonuspunkte, die Sie später einlösen können.' and referral line 'Profitieren Sie von Empfehlungsrabatten, wenn Sie unseren Shop weiterempfehlen.' verbatim with correctly scaled-down inline icons. 5 = all strings exact, boxes equal-sized and aligned, percent values in purple, badge logic correct. 3 = correct content with cosmetic unevenness (e.g. DANKE5 box bottom feels empty without the badge). 1 = wrong condition text, badge on the wrong code, or a € anywhere.
items.reference: A 5: both coupon boxes data-exact with the einmalig chip only on SHOP7, loyalty/referral lines verbatim with inline star and people icons — the layout makes the 7 %-first-order vs 5 %-follow-up logic instantly scannable.

items.question: Rate whether the back reads as a professional shop-benefits page overall: full-bleed navy header band with 'Ihre Vorteile im offiziellen Online-Shop' in white, balanced 2×3 grid, clean section dividers, bold repeated CTA, and the two-line light-weight legal footer — all collision-free and inside the safe zone.
items.guidance: The first composition FAILED here (loyalty/referral lines collided with a fixed footer, legal text overflowed to y=2469 vs the 2445 limit) and was rebuilt with measured flow. Verify the fix held: no overlap between the referral line and the purple hairline, footer 'Besuchen Sie uns jetzt: www.blausweta-rasur.de' bold-centered, legal line 'Codes nicht kombinierbar, pro Bestellung nur ein Code. SHOP7 nur einmal pro Kunde gültig. Alle Rabatte sind in Prozent.' breaking into two balanced lines, everything ≥5 mm inside trim except the deliberately full-bleed navy band. 5 = composed, hierarchical, draft_back's clutter fully exorcised. 3 = sound structure with uneven grid gutters or cramped descriptions. 1 = collisions or clipped text still present.
items.reference: A 5: navy band → 2×3 benefits → coupon pair → loyalty/referral → CTA + small print, in strict navy/purple/white with confident hierarchy; the previously-logged footer collision is gone and content ends at y=2419, inside the 2445 safe limit.

outputs.asked: Deliverable 1: print-ready two-sided PDF for SAXOPRINT — DIN A5 portrait, trim 148×210 mm, artwork 152×214 mm with 2 mm bleed, CMYK / PDF/X-4 preferred (delivered as RGB raster with disclosed limitation).

items.question: Does the PDF contain exactly 2 pages (page 1 = front, page 2 = back, identical artwork to the PNG previews) with crop marks at all four corners marking the 148×210 mm trim box, drawn outside the artwork with a gap so they don't intrude into the bleed?
items.guidance: Open the PDF and compare each page against front_152x214mm.png / back_152x214mm.png — same composition, same copy. At each corner expect two short hairline marks (horizontal + vertical) on the white slug margin, offset from the trim corner; they must not touch the 2 mm bleed area or overprint artwork.
items.reference: Yes — 2 pages matching the previews, with corner crop marks registering the 148×210 mm trim on a wider slug canvas, separated from the bleed by a visible gap.

items.question: From the PDF's page properties, report the page size in points and state whether it equals the documented 1895×2628 px slug at 300 dpi.
items.guidance: Read the MediaBox / document properties. Convert: px ÷ 300 dpi × 72 = pt, so 1895 px → 454.80 pt and 2628 px → 630.72 pt. The slug is intentionally larger than the 152×214 mm artwork to host the crop marks.
items.reference: 454.8 × 630.72 pt per page — exactly 1895×2628 px @300 dpi, i.e. the 152×214 mm artwork centered on a slug with room for trim marks.

items.question: The brief asked for CMYK PDF/X-4 with embedded/outlined fonts; the delivered file is an RGB raster PDF with text flattened into the image, and the README states CMYK/PDF-X-4 conversion is left to the print house. Rate the acceptability of this disclosed limitation for the SAXOPRINT submission.
items.guidance: Judge disclosure + practical printability, not just spec-matching. In favor: SAXOPRINT preflight does convert RGB input, the raster is a true 300 dpi with correct trim/bleed/marks, and the gap is stated plainly in README row 1 rather than hidden. Against: 'PDF/X-4 preferred' and 'embed all fonts' are acceptance criteria the file objectively does not meet, and rasterized text is a real downgrade for a print product. 5 = limitation fully disclosed AND the file would still print correctly via the print house's normal RGB→CMYK path. 3 = disclosed but a prepress operator would bounce it back for a proper X-4 source. 1 = the README had claimed compliance it doesn't have.
items.reference: Expected 3-4: geometry, resolution and marks are right and the README is candid that RGB-raster→CMYK/X-4 is a print-house or human-.ai step — honest and usable, but objectively short of the 'passes SAXOPRINT preflight as PDF/X-4' acceptance line.

items.question: Rate the raster quality of the PDF pages at print scale: is the 300 dpi flattening clean enough that body text (the 30 px-light legal line, the 37 px benefit descriptions) and the thin navy coupon outlines stay crisp at 100% A5 size?
items.guidance: Zoom both pages to actual print size. 5 = type edges crisp, no resampling ringing or JPEG blocking, hairlines (purple dividers, coupon box strokes) unbroken, photo band free of compression artifacts. 3 = slight softness in the lightest small text but legible. 1 = visible resampling blur or artifacting that would survive into print.
items.reference: A 5: the pages were embedded at native 1:1 resolution from the 1795×2528 px artwork, so the smallest text on the sheet — the two-line legal footer — remains sharp and the hairlines clean at 300 dpi.

outputs.asked: Supporting asset toward Deliverable 3 (editable source / 'real vector logo'): a true SVG vectorization of the official logo, produced via the connector's image_vectorize (Illustrator API), for the human .ai handoff.

items.question: Is this a genuine vector file — real <path> geometry (around 29 paths on a 1024×1024 canvas) reconstructing the razor motif and the 'Blausweta-Rasur' wordmark — rather than an SVG wrapper around an embedded bitmap?
items.guidance: Open the file as text: it must contain multiple <path d=...> elements and NO <image> tag with base64 data. Then render it scaled up (e.g. 400%) — edges must stay mathematically crisp, which a wrapped raster cannot do.
items.reference: Yes — ~29 vector paths, no embedded raster; the lockup scales without pixelation.

items.question: Rate the vectorization fidelity against logo_blausweta.png: letterform accuracy of 'Blausweta-Rasur' (including the hyphen and the bowl/counter shapes of a, e, u, R), the razor's blade/handle geometry with its two white pivot dots, and color fidelity to the navy/purple originals.
items.guidance: Auto-trace failure modes to hunt: lumpy or wobbling curve segments on letter bowls, filled-in counters (the enclosed whites of a/e/u/B/R), rounded-off corners on the blade, color quantization shifting #1E2D5C or #5B4A9E. Overlay or A/B the SVG render with the PNG at the same size. 5 = visually indistinguishable at print sizes, counters open, dots present. 3 = legible but with visible trace softness on close inspection. 1 = distorted glyphs or lost details.
items.reference: A 5: the trace preserves every glyph, the hyphen, open counters, the purple handle with both white pivot dots and the navy blade, in colors matching the brand hexes — usable wherever the PNG was.

items.question: Rate this SVG's fitness for its declared purpose — making the human .ai/.indd handoff trivial: is it import-ready for Illustrator, and does it have drawbacks a finisher must fix (e.g. a full-canvas white background path baked in as the first path, traced outlines instead of live type)?
items.guidance: Inspect the path list: the first path spans the whole 0,0→1024,1024 canvas (an opaque background rectangle), meaning the logo is NOT transparent-backed as delivered, and all lettering is outlines, not editable text — fine for 'fonts converted to outlines' print use, useless for retyping. 5 = clean layered paths, trivial to delete the background and group the mark. 3 = works but needs cleanup (background removal, path grouping, stray nodes). 1 = single merged blob path that defeats the handoff purpose.
items.reference: Expected 4: a real Illustrator-API vectorization that genuinely de-risks the handoff, with two known cleanup chores — delete the baked-in full-canvas background path and accept outlined (non-live) letterforms.

task_level.question: Score end-to-end deliverable coverage against the brief's four numbered deliverables: (1) print PDF for SAXOPRINT, (2) separate hi-res front/back previews, (3) editable .ai/.indd source with editable text, (4) safe-zone/bleed/250-300 dpi compliance — counting honest disclosure where a line could not be met.
task_level.guidance: Map each line to files: (1) insert_print_A5.pdf exists but is RGB raster, not CMYK/X-4 — disclosed; (2) front_152x214mm.png + back_152x214mm.png at exact 1795×2528 @300 dpi — fully met; (3) no .ai/.indd exists — declared a human handoff per the brief's own 'partial' feasibility, mitigated by the parametric compose_3437.py + vector SVG + processed work/ assets; (4) content asserted ≥5 mm inside trim, navy band full-bleed, no upscaling. 5 = every producible line produced at spec and every gap explicitly disclosed with a credible mitigation. 3 = deliverables present but a gap under-disclosed or mitigation thin. 1 = asked lines silently missing.
task_level.reference: Expected 4: previews and geometry are fully met, the two hard gaps (RGB-not-X-4 PDF, no .ai/.indd) are the connector's documented ceiling, disclosed in README rows 1 and 3 with concrete handoff mitigations — strong coverage, but two of four asked lines still need a human to truly close.

task_level.question: Campaign integrity across ALL pieces: do SHOP7 = 7 % (first direct order after switching, one-time) and DANKE5 = 5 % (follow-up order) appear consistently on front and back, with every discount expressed as a percentage, zero € signs anywhere, and zero leakage of the drafts' legacy offer 'RASUR-FREUNDE10 / 10%' or its 31.12.2024 expiry?
task_level.guidance: Scan front chips, back coupon boxes, loyalty/referral lines, both CTA lines and the legal footer on every output (both PNGs and both PDF pages). The codes must be spelled identically everywhere (SHOP7, DANKE5 — no hyphens, no lowercase), 7 % always with SHOP7 and 5 % always with DANKE5, and nothing from draft_back's coupon may survive.
task_level.reference: Yes — both codes are consistent across all four rendered surfaces, percentages only ('Alle Rabatte sind in Prozent.' in the footer), no €, and the legacy RASUR-FREUNDE10 offer appears nowhere.

task_level.question: Rate front↔back coherence as one two-sided piece: same navy #1E2D5C / purple #5B4A9E / white palette, same Helvetica Neue family and weight logic, the front's code chips visually foreshadowing the back's coupon boxes, the same purple hairline divider language, and 'Besuchen Sie uns jetzt: www.blausweta-rasur.de' anchoring both sides.
task_level.guidance: Flip between the two PNGs. 5 = unmistakably one designed object — identical hues (sample them), consistent corner radii and stroke weights between front chips and back coupon outlines, matching margins, the warm-front/professional-back contrast feeling intentional rather than disjointed. 3 = shared palette but mismatched component styling (e.g. chip radius vs coupon radius, differing hairline weights). 1 = the sides could be from different jobs.
task_level.reference: A 5: shared palette, type system, hairlines and centered CTA bind the warm thank-you front and the dense benefits back into one coherent insert — the front teases the codes, the back explains them.

task_level.question: Would the real client — Blausweta-Rasur's owner, who rejected their own AI drafts and is paying for a clean SAXOPRINT-ready rebuild — accept this package and pay?
task_level.guidance: Adopt the client's stated success test: a proper print layout that fixes the drafts' wedding-card front and clumsy back, exact codes, navy/purple discipline, files they can send to SAXOPRINT. 5 = accept and pay as-is, treating the disclosed RGB/.ai gaps as expected per the job's 'partial' feasibility note. 4 = accept with minor revision requests (real shield/package icons, transparent-background SVG). 3 = pay only after a human supplies the CMYK X-4 PDF and .ai source. 2 = dispute over missing contracted deliverables. 1 = reject outright.
task_level.reference: Expected 4: the design rebuild itself is exactly what was ordered and visibly superior to the drafts, codes and copy are flawless; the withheld payment risk sits entirely on the .ai source and true PDF/X-4 — both flagged upfront as human-handoff items.

task_level.question: Prohibitions audit across the final outputs: is there NO gold, NO green, NO Trustpilot badge, NO hearts/ornamental flourishes/handwritten romantic scripts, and is every rendered German string in the formal Sie-form (the drafts' Du-form 'FÜR DICH' / 'DEINER' register fully eliminated)?
task_level.guidance: Sweep both output PNGs and the PDF pages. Color check: the warehouse photo may contain natural wood/kraft tones (photographic content is exempt), but no DESIGN element may be gold or green; the drafts' yellow thumbs-up/gold gift box must have no descendants. Type check: every face is a clean sans; read all 34 rendered strings for informal address — the four added microcopy strings ('Ihre Vorteile im offiziellen Online-Shop', 'einmalig', '4', '5') must also be register-correct.
task_level.reference: Yes — design elements are exclusively navy/purple/white, no badges or romantic ornamentation anywhere, and all copy including the added microcopy uses the formal Sie-form ('Ihre Vorteile...').

task_level.question: Name the single most significant weakness of this delivery that you would flag to the client before they send files to SAXOPRINT, and say whether the freelancer disclosed it.
task_level.guidance: Pick ONE top issue and justify briefly. Strong candidates observed in this package: (a) the print PDF is flattened RGB raster — no live/outlined fonts, no CMYK, no PDF/X-4 — so the contractual 'passes SAXOPRINT preflight' criterion is unproven; (b) no editable .ai/.indd source at all, the brief's deliverable #1 by the client's own numbering; (c) the numbered-circle '4'/'5' fallback rows on the back breaking icon consistency. Weak answers cite cosmetic nits while missing the prepress gap.
task_level.reference: Expected: the RGB-raster, non-PDF/X-4 print file (equivalently: the missing editable .ai/.indd it depends on) — the one gap that blocks true print-readiness; it IS disclosed, candidly, in outputs/README.md rows 1 and 3 and the acceptance-criteria check.

tasks.title: Double-Sided 5x7 THC Beverage Postcard Design
tasks.one_line_task: Design a premium double-sided 5×7 trade postcard that sells THC beverages to bar and restaurant buyers using the approved copy deck, nine brand logos and two QR codes.
tasks.one_line_outputs: A print-ready double-sided 5×7 postcard PDF with bleed and crop marks plus hi-res front and back artwork files.
tasks.brief: DESCRIPTION: Need a designer to create a clean, modern, premium double-sided 5x7 postcard (with bleed) for a THC beverage distribution company. Final file should be print-ready for professional printing. Looking for someone with experience designing for beverage, alcohol, cannabis, hospitality, nightlife, or restaurant brands. Please include relevant portfolio examples with your proposal. Assets, logos, and product photos will be provided. TIMELINE: * Initial concepts within 24–48 hours * Final files delivered within 3 days SIZE: * 5” x 7” * Double-sided * Include bleed + crop marks * Horizontal or vertical layout (designer recommendation welcome) DELIVERABLES: * Print-ready PDF * Editable Canva file GOAL: Create a premium handout for bars, restaurants, liquor stores, and on-premise accounts to promote THC beverages as a high-margin revenue stream. STYLE: * Beverage-forward, modern, premium * Bold and easy to scan quickly * NOT psychedelic, “stoner,” or smoke-shop style * Should feel similar to alcohol/hospitality marketing FRONT SIDE CONTENT: Headline: SERVE WHAT’S NEXT Subhead: Add a high-margin revenue stream with THC drinks. No alcohol. No hangover. 21+. Made with hemp - marijuana’s milder cousin - for a lighter, more buildable buzz. Stat: Alcohol consumption in the U.S. is at a 90-year low. Supporting line: Consumers still want social experiences - just different options. Business bullets: * High-margin pours * Repeat purchase behavior * Easy to add to existing menus QR Code Section: Scan for Product & Pricing Guide BACK SIDE CONTENT: Headline: A NEW KIND OF BUZZ Sections: * Spirit Bottles → for shots + cocktails (“Hightails”) * RTD Cans → ready-to-drink social beverages * THC Drops → turn any alcohol-free drink into a THC drink Additional points: * Delta-9 THC * Hemp-derived * Legal in Missouri --- This is a key point we need to get across*** * Social alternative to alcohol * Multiple mg/dose options available Brands/logos to include: * HiSide * Howdy * THC Social * Stay Cool * SUP * 8th Wonder * Tempters * CURRNT * Don’t Be That Dude QR CODE SECTION: WHAT’S THE BUZZ ABOUT? THC Drinks Explained - Scan to Watch. IMPORTANT: Need clean visual hierarchy, modern typography, product/lifestyle imagery, logo placement, and space for QR code.
