tasks.readme:
# 3252 — Arch & Prairie Beverage Co. 5"x7" THC-Drinks Trade Postcard (deliverables)

Designer recommendation taken: **landscape 7x5** — the stronger hierarchy for a bar-top
leave-behind (headline column left, pour imagery right, QR at the natural thumb position).

## Asked → produced

| Asked (TASK.md) | Produced file | Exact px / dpi | How it was made |
|---|---|---|---|
| Double-sided 5x7 postcard, print-ready PDF with 0.125" bleed + crop marks | `postcard_print_5x7.pdf` (2 pages: front, back) | 2275x1675 px slug pages @300dpi (7.583x5.583"); artwork 2175x1575 (7.25x5.25" incl. 0.125" bleed); crop marks at the 2100x1500 (7x5") trim, offset outside the bleed | Adobe connector element processing (15 ops: 10 background removals, auto-tone, +vibrance chain, warm color-temperature, 7:2 smart crop) + local PIL composition; PDF assembled locally because the connector has no image→PDF tool |
| Front artwork | `front_7x5.png` | 2175x1575 @300dpi | Full-bleed connector-graded bar pour, charcoal-green scrim, verbatim JSON copy (headline split SERVE / WHAT’S NEXT, curly apostrophe intact), amber stat callout, copper-dot bullets, wordmark chip, pricing-guide QR at 480px on cream panel |
| Back artwork | `back_7x5.png` | 2175x1575 @300dpi | Header + copper hairline, connector 7:2 cooler strip, three product sections (single didot-italic "Hightails" flourish), 5 key-point pills with **Legal in Missouri** amber-emphasized, 9-brand logo wall (3x3, white panel, JSON order), explainer QR at native 444px on white panel + warmed patio social-proof thumb, compliance fine print |
| Editable source file (Adobe Express) | not produced — human handoff | — | Per the project decision, the editable Express/Canva source is a human-designer handoff step; `compose_3252.py` (parametric, reads all copy from `postcard_copy.json`) is the regenerable source of record in the meantime — rotate SKUs by editing the JSON and re-running |
| Layout recommendation | landscape 7x5 (this delivery) | — | Stronger 5-second hierarchy; rationale above |
| Concepts in 24–48h, finals in 3 days | this package is the concept+final candidate | — | Delivered same-day; within both the 24–48h concept window and the 3-day final window |

## Copy & acceptance checks (verified programmatically + visually)

- All copy rendered **verbatim from `postcard_copy.json`** (curly quotes/apostrophes intact; no retyped strings). The only non-JSON visual element is the drawn copper arrow after each product-section name (Futura has no U+2192 glyph) — a layout connective, not copy.
- "Legal in Missouri" is the single amber-filled, bold, larger pill among the five key points.
- All 9 brand marks placed in the JSON `brands` order, connector-cut, legible at print size (cells ~1.39"x0.45" at 300dpi).
- Both QR codes are the real supplied codes: front 480px (NEAREST integer-ish upscale of the 444px original), back placed **bit-identical at native 444px** (verified). Quiet zones ≥4 modules of light ground (24px white internal border + ≥40px cream/white panel padding).
- Compliance line (21+ / hemp-derived / 2018 Farm Bill / Missouri law) runs as fine print along the bottom of the back, inside the trim.

## Honest limitations

- **PDF/X-4 prepress conversion** (ICC profiles, CMYK separation) is a print-house step; this PDF is RGB @300dpi with bleed + trim crop marks.
- **Editable Adobe Express source**: not generated by this pipeline (see table) — flagged for human handoff.
- The compliance fine print sits 89px from the artwork edge (inside the trim, slightly inside the usual 0.25" safe zone) by design, as the brief asks for fine print "along the bottom edge".
- Photography is used at its supplied resolution (1536px sources cover-cropped; no upscaling claimed as print-res).

input_assets.what_it_is: The client-approved, word-for-word copy deck for both sides of the postcard — every string on the card (headlines, subhead, stat, bullets, sections, key points, brands, QR labels/URLs, compliance line) must be transcribed from this file, not rewritten.

items.question: In postcard_copy.json, does the key_points array contain exactly five entries (Delta-9 THC; Hemp-derived; Legal in Missouri; Social alternative to alcohol; Multiple mg/dose options available.) with emphasize=true set ONLY on "Legal in Missouri" and emphasize=false on the other four?
items.guidance: Open the JSON and read the key_points array literally. The client said Legal in Missouri 'is THE key point we need to get across', so the emphasis flag must travel with the data — a missing or extra emphasize=true would mis-style the printed pills downstream.
items.reference: Yes — five entries in that order, emphasize=true only on "Legal in Missouri". Note the fifth point carries a trailing period ("Multiple mg/dose options available.").

items.question: How faithful is the copy deck to the client work order, character for character — including curly punctuation and the nine brand spellings?
items.guidance: Spot-check the risky strings: front headline "SERVE WHAT’S NEXT" must use a curly apostrophe (’, not '); the subhead must read "...Made with hemp - marijuana’s milder cousin - for a lighter, more buildable buzz." with plain hyphens; back_sections[0].desc must keep curly double quotes around “Hightails”; back.qr_label must read "WHAT’S THE BUZZ ABOUT? THC Drinks Explained - Scan to Watch"; the brands array must spell CURRNT (six letters, no E), Don’t Be That Dude (curly apostrophe), HiSide (capital S), 8th Wonder (numeral 8). 5 = every string verbatim including punctuation; 3 = one or two minor punctuation slips that would survive print without client pushback; 1 = paraphrased or misspelled brand/headline copy.
items.reference: A 5: every string matches. Verified verbatim: curly apostrophes in SERVE WHAT’S NEXT / marijuana’s / WHAT’S THE BUZZ ABOUT? / Don’t Be That Dude, curly quotes around “Hightails”, all nine brands spelled exactly, compliance_line reads "21+ only. Hemp-derived Delta-9 THC compliant with the 2018 Farm Bill and Missouri law. Enjoy responsibly."

items.question: Comparing postcard_copy.json against the copy specified in the work order (TASK.md) and the original Upwork brief, list any wording or punctuation divergences you can find (or write 'none').
items.guidance: Diff string by string. Pay attention to terminal punctuation on the fifth key point and on the back QR label — they are the only places the sources disagree.
items.reference: Two sub-character deltas only: (1) key_points[4] "Multiple mg/dose options available." carries a trailing period the original brief bullet lacked; (2) the JSON back.qr_label ends "Scan to Watch" with no trailing period, while TASK.md quotes the label as "...Scan to Watch." Everything else, including all nine brand spellings and both URLs (archandprairie.com/trade/product-pricing-guide and /whats-the-buzz/thc-drinks-explained), matches.

input_assets.what_it_is: The distributor's own logo (1024x1024, gemini-3-pro-image) — per the brief it 'anchors the front' of the postcard, placed top-left on a cream chip after background removal.

items.question: Does the wordmark read exactly "ARCH & PRAIRIE" over "BEVERAGE CO." (period after CO present) in deep-green serif capitals, with a gold coupe/cocktail-glass motif sitting between the ampersand and "PRAIRIE"?
items.guidance: Read every letter — generated logos often drop the period after Co. or garble PRAIRIE. Confirm P-R-A-I-R-I-E is complete and the glass is a separate motif, not a replacement for a letter. (The pipeline's own QC bot wrongly claimed the period was missing — check the pixels yourself.)
items.reference: Yes — both lines are spelled correctly, "CO." includes the period, and the gold coupe with stem sits between "&" and "PRAIRIE" without deleting any letter.

items.question: How well does this wordmark sell 'craft-spirits importer, not dispensary' — the premium hospitality positioning the brief demands?
items.guidance: The persona palette is deep charcoal-green #18271F + gold/copper accents. Look for: classic serif caps, restrained two-color scheme, a drinks-trade motif (coupe glass), and zero cannabis iconography (no leaf, no smoke). 5 = could sit on a fine-spirits price book unchanged; 3 = premium-ish but with one cheapening element (e.g. the gradient on the glass); 1 = reads like a smoke-shop or clip-art mark.
items.reference: A 5: dark-green serif caps with a gold coupe motif, flat white background, no THC visual cues at all — convincingly a hospitality-trade brand. The slight gold gradient on the glass is the only deviation from a flat premium finish.

items.question: How suitable is this file for the Adobe workflow it feeds — background removal, then placement at roughly 396 px wide on a cream rounded chip?
items.guidance: Check for cutout hazards: is the background pure flat white (it should be); do the thin serif strokes and the slim glass stem stay legible when the 1024 px mark is scaled to chip size; would the gold gradient band on the coupe leave fringing after auto background removal? 5 = clean cut and fully legible at chip size; 3 = legible but fine strokes go fragile; 1 = letters or stem break up.
items.reference: A 5: flat white ground cuts cleanly; on the final front the chip version is crisp and readable, with both text lines and the coupe intact at small size.

input_assets.what_it_is: The nine portfolio brand marks the distributor carries — all must appear, legible, in the back-of-card logo wall after background removal.

items.question: Transcribe the wordmark text of each of the nine logo files in order (hiside, howdy, thc_social, stay_cool, sup, 8th_wonder, tempters, currnt, dont_be_that_dude) and flag any that do not match the brief's brand list.
items.guidance: The brief list is: HiSide, Howdy, THC Social, Stay Cool, SUP, 8th Wonder, Tempters, CURRNT, Don’t Be That Dude. Two were flagged by the pipeline's QC bot as possible misreads — the rope-script mark (claimed to read 'Heady') and the red script mark (claimed 'Templers'). Read them carefully yourself; script and rope letterforms are the failure-prone ones. Also confirm CURRNT has no E and Don’t keeps its apostrophe.
items.reference: All nine match: HiSide (navy, gray underline swoosh), Howdy (tan rope script — it does read Howdy, not Heady), THC Social (charcoal + teal speech bubble), Stay Cool (navy condensed with light-blue outline), SUP (teal caps over a tan wave), 8th Wonder (black/gold art-deco), Tempters (red/black script with devil-tail arrow — not 'Templers'), CURRNT (charcoal caps, six letters, yellow zigzag through the middle), Don’t Be That Dude (black/orange stacked, apostrophe present).

items.question: Does the CURRNT mark spell exactly C-U-R-R-N-T — six letters with NO letter E — with a yellow-green zigzag line running through the letterforms?
items.guidance: This is the easiest brand to silently 'auto-correct' to CURRENT. Count the letters in the image. The style hint is 'Bold Contemporary'; the zigzag is part of the identity, not a defect.
items.reference: Yes — CURRNT, six letters, no E, with the chartreuse zigzag crossing the R-R-N region.

items.question: As a set, how usable are these nine marks for a 3x3 logo wall printed at cells of roughly 1.4 x 0.45 inches — considering cutout cleanliness and small-size legibility?
items.guidance: Judge the worst offenders, not the average: HiSide's thin geometric strokes, Howdy's intricate rope texture, 8th Wonder's fine deco linework, Stay Cool's outline/shadow, Tempters' script flourishes. All nine sit on flat white (good for image_remove_background). 5 = every mark survives at cell size with clean edges; 3 = one or two go mushy or thin but stay identifiable; 1 = several become illegible or cut badly.
items.reference: A 4 is honest: all nine cut cleanly off white and remain identifiable on the final wall, but Howdy's rope detail and 8th Wonder's fine gold lines are at the edge of what 1.4-inch print cells can hold, and HiSide's thin strokes print light.

input_assets.display: Lifestyle photography set (bar pour, cooler lineup, patio toast)
input_assets.what_it_is: Three gpt-image-2 lifestyle photos (1536x1024): the front hero (bartender pouring an unbranded can), the back header strip source (glowing can cooler), and the back social-proof thumb (three friends toasting cans at a patio dinner).

items.question: How photoreal are the people and surfaces — would a bar GM holding the printed card clock these as AI?
items.guidance: Zoom into the failure zones: the bartender's beard and curly hair strands, his hands and knuckles around the silver can, the pour stream into the rocks glass; in patio_toast, the three faces mid-laugh, the woman's gold hoop earrings and the ring on the right woman's hand, fingers wrapped around three cans clinked together, the noodle plates; in cooler_lineup, can-to-can texture repetition and condensation. 5 = passes as commercial stock at print size; 3 = one visible tell under scrutiny; 1 = obvious AI artifacts (fused fingers, melted text, impossible reflections).
items.reference: A 4-5: all three are strong — believable skin, hair and bokeh string lights. The pipeline judge scored bar_pour 10, patio_toast 10, cooler_lineup 9 (its one nitpick: subtle uniformity of sheen on the far-left cans).

items.question: Are ALL cans visible across the three photos deliberately unbranded — no readable labels, logos or text on any can (per the brief: 'brand marks live in the logo wall, not on the cans')?
items.guidance: Check the silver can in the bartender's hand, every can on the cooler's four shelves (dozens, in many colors), and the three white cans being toasted on the patio. Any legible pseudo-brand or garbled AI text on a can is a fail — it would collide with the real nine-brand portfolio on the card's back.
items.reference: Yes — every can is a clean unlabeled cylinder (matte colors in the cooler, plain silver/white in the hands). No readable or pseudo text on any can.

items.question: How well do these three photos fit the specific layout jobs the plan assigns them — bar_pour as a full-bleed front with a dark left scrim and type column, cooler_lineup as a 7:2 header strip, patio_toast as a small warm thumbnail?
items.guidance: bar_pour: is its left side dark/uncluttered enough to take a #18271F scrim plus cream headline type, with the subject weighted right? cooler_lineup: do the glowing shelves survive a brutal 7:2 horizontal crop (the interesting content spans the full height, so much must go)? patio_toast: does it still read 'social proof' when shrunk to ~300 px wide? 5 = all three slot in with no compromise; 3 = one asset fights its crop; 1 = a photo is unusable for its assigned role.
items.reference: A 4: bar_pour is near-ideal (dark left, subject right); patio_toast thumbnails well; cooler_lineup is the compromise — a true 7:2 reframe keeps only one shelf-band of cans (the connector's first crop attempt even fell back to pillarboxing before a retry with point focus succeeded).

input_assets.display: Two real QR codes (pricing guide + explainer video)
input_assets.what_it_is: Deterministic, genuinely scannable 444x444 QR codes — front links to the product & pricing guide, back to the 'What's the Buzz' explainer video; the cardinal rule is they get placed, never redrawn.

items.question: Scanning both PNGs with a phone, does qr_front_pricing.png resolve to https://archandprairie.com/trade/product-pricing-guide and qr_back_video.png to https://archandprairie.com/whats-the-buzz/thc-drinks-explained (the urls recorded in postcard_copy.json qr_links)?
items.guidance: Actually scan them from the screen. The two codes must be distinct (they are visually different patterns) and must match their side: pricing on front, video on back. A swapped pair would route bar managers to the wrong asset.
items.reference: Yes — two distinct codes encoding exactly those two archandprairie.com URLs, front=pricing-guide, back=thc-drinks-explained.

items.question: How print-robust are these QR files as supplied — module crispness, contrast, and built-in quiet zone at 444x444 px?
items.guidance: Zoom to pixel level: modules should be hard-edged pure black on pure white (no anti-aliasing soup, no JPEG ringing), with a white quiet-zone border of at least 4 modules baked into the file. At 444 px placed near 1.5 inches on a 300dpi card, module size is comfortable. 5 = crisp binary modules + generous quiet zone; 3 = scannable but soft edges or tight quiet zone; 1 = blurry or quiet zone missing.
items.reference: A 5: both are clean library-generated codes — sharp black modules, white ground, visible white margin on all four sides.

outputs.asked: The front face of the double-sided 5x7 postcard — the 5-second 'high-margin revenue stream' pitch with headline, stat, bullets, wordmark and pricing-guide QR.

items.question: Is every front copy block rendered verbatim from postcard_copy.json: headline "SERVE WHAT’S NEXT" (split SERVE / WHAT’S NEXT with a curly apostrophe), the full subhead ending "...for a lighter, more buildable buzz.", stat "Alcohol consumption in the U.S. is at a 90-year low.", supporting line "Consumers still want social experiences - just different options.", and the three bullets High-margin pours / Repeat purchase behavior / Easy to add to existing menus?
items.guidance: Read each block on the artwork against the JSON character by character. Watch the two traps: a straightened apostrophe in WHAT’S, and any 'helpful' rewriting of the subhead's plain hyphens. Bullet order matters.
items.reference: Yes — all blocks verbatim. The headline apostrophe is curly; the subhead wraps over four lines with the exact hyphen phrasing; the amber callout carries the 90-year-low stat word for word; bullets appear in JSON order with copper dot markers.

items.question: Does the front deliver the brief's 5-second arm's-length read — 'make a GM see a high-margin revenue stream in five seconds, then scan a QR code'?
items.guidance: Squint test at thumbnail size: the eye should land on SERVE WHAT’S NEXT, then the amber stat bar, then the QR panel. Check craft details: headline left-aligned in the scrim column without touching the photo's bright zones, the amber rule + amber bold stat visibly outranking the body copy, the three bullets aligned with even copper dots, the cream QR panel separated from the bullets (the run logged the left column bottoming at 1446 px vs a 1462 px safe limit — verify nothing collides or clips at the bottom edge). 5 = instant hierarchy, no collisions; 3 = readable but one weak link (e.g. supporting line crowds bullets); 1 = competing focal points or clipped content.
items.reference: A 5 reads: headline -> amber stat -> bullets -> QR in that order at a glance, with the wordmark chip quietly anchoring top-left and clear space everywhere. The delivered front largely achieves this; the subhead is long but subordinated.

items.question: How well does the treatment of the bar_pour hero photo serve the card — given that the cream QR panel is placed exactly over the region where the bartender's can-pour and glass sit in the source photo?
items.guidance: Compare with input_assets/bar_pour.jpg: in the source, the action is a silver can pouring into a rocks glass. On the final front, judge what survives: you see the bartender's face looking down and the warm backbar, but the pour itself is hidden behind the QR panel, so the image reads 'bartender at work', not 'pouring a THC drink'. Decide how much that costs a card whose product story is the pour. 5 = hero action visible and composition feels intentional; 3 = action obscured but the photo still sells premium bar atmosphere; 1 = the layout visibly buries the point of the photo (e.g. amputated hands or floating glass artifacts).
items.reference: Expect a 3-4: the scrim/typography integration and color grade (connector auto-tone + vibrance) are genuinely premium and nothing looks broken, but the one action moment — the pour — sits behind the QR panel, a real composition trade-off a senior designer might have avoided by mirroring the layout or nudging the crop.

items.question: Transcribe the label under the front QR code exactly as printed (including line breaks), and state the color of the panel it sits on.
items.guidance: Zoom into the bottom-right panel. The JSON string is "Scan for Product & Pricing Guide" — check the ampersand survived and nothing was appended (no period, no 'today!').
items.reference: "Scan for Product &" / "Pricing Guide" — two centered lines, dark charcoal-green bold type on a cream (#F4EDDC-family) rounded panel, no trailing punctuation.

outputs.asked: The back face — 'A NEW KIND OF BUZZ' information side: three product sections, five key-point pills with Legal in Missouri emphasized, the nine-brand logo wall, explainer-video QR, and compliance fine print.

items.question: Is "Legal in Missouri" the single visually emphasized key point — the only pill with a solid amber (#DCA54C) fill and bold charcoal-green text, while the other four (Delta-9 THC, Hemp-derived, Social alternative to alcohol, Multiple mg/dose options available.) are thin copper-outline pills?
items.guidance: This is the client's number-one acceptance criterion ('THE key point we need to get across'). Count the pills: there must be exactly five (row of three + row of two), and exactly one amber-filled. Also confirm the fifth pill keeps its trailing period from the JSON.
items.reference: Yes — five pills, with Legal in Missouri as the sole amber-filled, bold, slightly larger pill (third in the top row); the other four are outline-only; "Multiple mg/dose options available." prints with its period.

items.question: Read the 3x3 logo wall and list the nine brands in row-major order (left to right, top to bottom).
items.guidance: The acceptance criteria require all nine marks placed and legible at print size, and the plan pins the order to the JSON brands array. Name each mark from the pixels — if any cell is ambiguous to you at print scale, say which.
items.reference: Row 1: HiSide, Howdy, THC Social. Row 2: Stay Cool, SUP, 8th Wonder. Row 3: Tempters, CURRNT, Don’t Be That Dude. (Exactly the JSON order; all nine on one white rounded panel.)

items.question: Rate the back's information architecture and copy craft: A NEW KIND OF BUZZ header over a copper hairline, the 7:2 cooler photo strip, three product columns (Spirit Bottles / RTD Cans / THC Drops) with arrows and verbatim descriptions, the “Hightails” flourish, and the compliance fine print.
items.guidance: Check each: (a) section descriptions verbatim — "for shots + cocktails (“Hightails”)", "ready-to-drink social beverages", "turn any alcohol-free drink into a THC drink"; (b) the arrows after the section names are drawn copper arrows (the run replaced Futura's missing U+2192 glyph with vector arrows — they should look uniform, not tofu boxes); (c) “Hightails” set in a copper Didot italic as the single typographic flourish; (d) the bottom line reads exactly "21+ only. Hemp-derived Delta-9 THC compliant with the 2018 Farm Bill and Missouri law. Enjoy responsibly." in one uncut line. 5 = all verbatim, columns aligned, strip well-cropped, fine print clean; 3 = copy verbatim but one craft flaw (ragged column alignment, cramped strip); 1 = missing/paraphrased copy or rendering artifacts.
items.reference: A 5: all three descriptions verbatim with uniform drawn copper arrows, the cooler strip showing a glowing band of multicolor cans full-width under the header, “Hightails” in copper italic serif inside curly quotes, and the exact compliance sentence centered along the bottom edge. (Known disclosed nuance: the fine print sits about 89 px from the artwork edge — inside trim but tighter than the usual 0.25-inch safe zone.)

items.question: How well does the back QR zone work: the explainer code at its native size on a white panel with the exact three-line label, plus the warmed patio-toast thumbnail as social proof?
items.guidance: The label must read "WHAT’S THE BUZZ ABOUT? THC Drinks Explained - Scan to Watch" (curly apostrophe, capital WHAT’S line first). Per the run log the code is placed bit-identical at 444 px — judge what you can see: no rescaling blur, hard module edges, quiet zone of at least 4 modules of white/cream on all sides, and the rounded patio thumbnail below adding warmth without crowding the code. Try scanning the printed-size code off screen. 5 = scans instantly, label verbatim, panel uncluttered; 3 = scannable but tight margins or label line-broken awkwardly; 1 = blurred/rescaled code or wrong label.
items.reference: A 5: crisp native-resolution code on a white rounded panel, generous quiet zone, label verbatim over three lines ("WHAT’S THE BUZZ ABOUT?" / "THC Drinks Explained - Scan" / "to Watch"), patio thumbnail neatly rounded beneath.

outputs.asked: Deliverable 1: the double-sided 5x7 print-ready PDF with 0.125-inch bleed and crop marks; the README documents the asked-vs-produced mapping, including the editable-source handoff.

items.question: Does the PDF contain exactly two pages (front, then back) on an oversized slug — pages of 546 x 402 pt (7.583 x 5.583 in) — with corner crop marks marking a 7 x 5 inch trim, the marks offset OUTSIDE the 0.125-inch bleed, and artwork color extending past the trim line on all four sides?
items.guidance: Open the PDF at high zoom on each corner: you should see a white slug border, short horizontal+vertical tick marks that do not touch the artwork, and the charcoal-green/cream artwork bleeding past where the tick marks indicate the cut. Confirm page 1 is the SERVE WHAT’S NEXT front and page 2 the cream back. Marks overlapping the bleed area would risk printing inside the finished card.
items.reference: Yes — 2 pages at 546x402 pt, four corner tick pairs per page aligned to the 7x5 trim, marks clear of the bleed, and the artwork (identical to front_7x5.png / back_7x5.png at 2175x1575 @300dpi) bleeding 0.125 in beyond trim.

items.question: The run discloses that this PDF was assembled locally with PIL (the Adobe connector has no image-to-PDF tool) and is RGB raster at 300dpi — no PDF/X-4, no ICC/CMYK separation, no vector type. Given the brief asked for a 'print-ready PDF for professional printing', how acceptable is this file as delivered?
items.guidance: Judge like a print buyer: 300dpi raster with bleed + crop marks is accepted by most short-run digital printers (they convert RGB in their RIP), but offset/prepress shops expect CMYK PDF/X with vector text for crisp small type — the 26 px compliance line is the most exposure-sensitive element. Weigh that the limitation is disclosed honestly in README 'Honest limitations' rather than hidden. 5 = perfectly adequate for the postcard's realistic print path, disclosure complete; 3 = usable but the client would likely incur a prepress conversion step; 1 = misrepresented as press-ready or technically broken.
items.reference: A 3-4 is the honest range: dimensionally correct (bleed, trim marks, 300dpi) and transparently documented, but RGB raster-only — fine for a digital gang-run printer, short of true prepress PDF/X with live type.

items.question: Does README.md honestly and completely map every asked deliverable to what was produced — in particular, is the missing editable Adobe Express/Canva source clearly flagged as a human-handoff gap rather than glossed over?
items.guidance: The work order asked for (1) print-ready PDF, (2) editable source for SKU rotation, (3) an orientation recommendation, (4) the 24-48h/3-day timeline. Check the README's asked->produced table covers all four, states 'not produced — human handoff' for the Express source, offers compose_3252.py + postcard_copy.json as the interim regenerable source, gives the landscape-7x5 recommendation with a rationale, and notes same-day delivery within both windows. 5 = complete, accurate, nothing oversold; 3 = mostly accurate with one vague row; 1 = claims something exists that doesn't.
items.reference: A 5: the README table covers all asked rows, explicitly marks the editable Express source 'not produced — human handoff', explains SKU rotation via editing postcard_copy.json and re-running compose_3252.py, and lists four honest limitations (RGB PDF, no Express source, 89 px fine-print clearance, 1536 px photo sources).

task_level.question: End to end: would Arch & Prairie Beverage Co. accept this package and pay the invoice, measured against their seven acceptance criteria (verbatim copy, Legal-in-Missouri emphasis, 9 legible logos, both QRs with exact labels, 5x7 + bleed + crop marks + PDF + editable source, premium hospitality look with compliance fine print, timeline)?
task_level.guidance: Score the contract, not the aesthetics alone. Six of seven criteria are checkable on the pixels/PDF; the seventh (editable source) is explicitly not delivered — decide how fatal that is for a client who said 'our team can update when SKUs rotate'. 5 = pay in full, no revisions; 4 = pay after one minor revision round; 3 = accept conditionally (e.g. demands the Express file before final payment); 2 = major rework; 1 = reject.
task_level.reference: A 3-4: copy fidelity, Missouri emphasis, logo wall, QRs, dimensions/marks and styling are all satisfied; the one open contractual item is the editable Express/Canva source (disclosed as human handoff), which a real client would likely require before closing out the job.

task_level.question: Is every one of the 15 supplied visual assets actually used in the final card? (distributor logo -> front chip; bar_pour -> front full-bleed; cooler_lineup -> back header strip; patio_toast -> back thumbnail; all nine brand marks -> logo wall; qr_front_pricing -> front panel; qr_back_video -> back panel.)
task_level.guidance: Walk the list against both sides. The brief named front lead imagery from all three photos — the execution split them front/back instead, which is a defensible reading, but nothing the client supplied should be simply dropped.
task_level.reference: Yes — all 15 assets appear exactly once each across the two faces, in the placements listed.

task_level.question: Does the finished card hit the style mandate: premium alcohol/hospitality marketing ('closer to a craft-spirits importer than a dispensary') with strictly NO psychedelic, stoner or smoke-shop cues, on the approved palette (#18271F charcoal-green, #F4EDDC cream, #B97A36 copper, #DCA54C amber)?
task_level.guidance: Scan both faces for forbidden tropes: cannabis leaves, smoke wisps, tie-dye, trippy gradients, goofy THC puns set in display type — there should be zero. Then sample the actual colors of the scrim, back ground, pills, rules and stat callout against the four hexes. 5 = could be mistaken for a fine-spirits trade piece, palette faithful; 3 = premium overall but one off-palette or tonally giggly element; 1 = reads dispensary/smoke-shop.
task_level.reference: A 5: dark-green scrim + cream + copper/amber accents throughout, Futura-family hierarchy, no cannabis iconography anywhere — the only THC visuals are the word itself and the brand marks the client mandated.

task_level.question: Do front and back cohere as one premium piece — shared palette, type system, rounded-panel language, photo grading and margins — such that both sides obviously belong to the same card?
task_level.guidance: Flip between the two faces: the cream of the back ground should match the front's chip and QR panel; copper accents (front bullet dots, back arrows/hairline/pill outlines) should be the same hue; amber is reserved for emphasis on both sides (stat callout / Missouri pill); corner radii of chip, panels and thumbnails should feel like one family; the connector grading (auto-toned pour, warmed patio, glowing cooler) should share a warm cast. 5 = seamless system; 3 = clearly related but with one inconsistency (e.g. mismatched radii or label sizes); 1 = two different designers.
task_level.reference: A 5: identical palette and Futura hierarchy on both faces, consistent rounded cream/white panels, copper used for all connective accents and amber strictly for the two emphasis moments.

task_level.question: Name the single most consequential weakness of this delivery — the one thing a paying client would push back on first — and say in one sentence how you would fix it.
task_level.guidance: Pick ONE and justify briefly. Strong candidates you should weigh: the contractually-promised editable Adobe Express source not being produced (only a Python script + JSON as interim source); the RGB raster-only PDF vs true prepress PDF/X; the front QR panel covering the bar_pour photo's actual pour action; the compliance line sitting 89 px from the artwork edge (tighter than the 0.25-inch safe zone).
task_level.reference: Best answer: the missing editable Express/Canva source — it is an explicit deliverable tied to the client's stated workflow ('update when SKUs rotate'), and the fix is to rebuild the layout as an Express template (or at minimum a layered file) before final handoff. Also fully creditable: the QR panel hiding the hero pour (fix: mirror the panel to bottom-left or re-crop the photo), or the RGB-only PDF (fix: export PDF/X-4 with CMYK + live type).

tasks.title: Meta Ads Designer Needed for 3 Parenting Retargeting Static Creatives
tasks.task_type: Meta Retargeting Static Creatives — TeenTalk (3 concepts x3 sizes)
tasks.one_line_task: Create three emotionally credible static Meta retargeting concepts for TeenTalk and deliver each in three placement formats with the exact brand hierarchy.
tasks.one_line_outputs: Nine final ad images — each concept at 1080×1920, 1080×1350 and 1080×1080 — ready for Meta Ads Manager.
tasks.brief: We are looking for a direct-response Meta ads designer to create 3 static retargeting ad creatives for TeenTalk, a parenting support system for parents of teens. These are not generic social media posts or cold ads. They are bridge creatives for a video-qualified audience — designed to move warm parents from recognition into our story-driven landing page. The audience has already watched 50%+ of our video ads. The goal is to move warm parents from recognition into action and bring them to the landing page with the right expectation. The creative should make the right parent feel: “This is about me, my teen, my pattern at home — and there may be a real way forward.” Target audience: - English-speaking mothers - Parents of a teen / preteen - Emotionally tired, relationship-driven, and aware there is a real problem at home - They do not want random parenting advice anymore - They are looking for a real system, not one more tip Final deliverables: - 3 static Meta ad creative concepts - Each concept delivered in 3 formats: 1) 9:16 — 1080×1920 — Stories / Reels 2) 4:5 — 1080×1350 — Feed 3) 1:1 — 1080×1080 — Square / flexible placements - Total: 9 exported final image files - Editable source files included if possible: Canva, Figma, Photoshop or Illustrator - Commercial-use / royalty-free visuals only - Clean, readable, mobile-first design - Small TeenTalk logo / purple round stamp in the top corner of each creative - 1 reasonable revision round included - If the final designs do not follow the approved brief, those issues should be corrected Design direction: - Clean, emotional, modern, readable - Serious, warm, credible — not cheesy - Use real emotional mother/teen imagery - Avoid overly staged “perfect family” images - Avoid childish design - Avoid generic therapy-clinic style - Avoid aggressive clickbait - Main text must be readable on mobile - Do not overload with too much text - Strong visual hierarchy: emotional visual, headline, short supporting line, CTA block, small TeenTalk mark Brand direction: Use a clean, premium, emotionally intelligent style. TeenTalk brand colors include soft teal / turquoise, purple, and warm yellow/gold CTA accents. The design should feel connected to a modern parent-support product, not like a generic parenting quote post. The full creative copy and detailed direction for all 3 ads will be provided after hiring. Budget: Up to $120 fixed price for 3 static creative concepts delivered in 3 formats each. To apply, please send: 1. 2–3 examples of Meta / Facebook / Instagram static ad creatives you designed 2. Confirmation that you can deliver 9:16, 4:5 and 1:1 versions 3. Confirmation that editable source files are included if possible 4. Your estimated delivery time 5. Please start your proposal with the word “TeenTalk” so I know you read the full brief

tasks.readme:
# TeenTalk — Retargeting Statics (task 5388): 3 concepts x 3 formats = 9 finals

## Asked -> produced

| Asked (brief) | Produced file | px / dpi | How it was made |
|---|---|---|---|
| Concept 1 "Same old dynamic" — 9:16 Stories/Reels | `c1_story_1080x1920.png` | 1080x1920, sRGB PNG | Adobe connector `image_apply_auto_tone` on the supplied vertical master, connector `image_crop_and_resize` to 9:16 (both-people focus), local PIL composition: scrim + teal rule + verbatim type + gold CTA chip + stamp |
| Concept 1 — 4:5 Feed | `c1_feed_1080x1350.png` | 1080x1350, sRGB PNG | Same toned master, connector 4:5 crop, same layout system |
| Concept 1 — 1:1 Square | `c1_square_1080x1080.png` | 1080x1080, sRGB PNG | Connector auto-tone on the supplied square alternate frame, same layout system |
| Concept 2 "Growing silent gaps" — 9:16 | `c2_story_1080x1920.png` | 1080x1920, sRGB PNG | As concept 1 (toned vertical master, connector 9:16 crop) |
| Concept 2 — 4:5 | `c2_feed_1080x1350.png` | 1080x1350, sRGB PNG | As concept 1 (connector 4:5 crop) |
| Concept 2 — 1:1 | `c2_square_1080x1080.png` | 1080x1080, sRGB PNG | Toned square frame; stamp top-LEFT (see stamp rule note) |
| Concept 3 "A new framework" — 9:16 | `c3_story_1080x1920.png` | 1080x1920, sRGB PNG | As concept 1 |
| Concept 3 — 4:5 | `c3_feed_1080x1350.png` | 1080x1350, sRGB PNG | As concept 1; stamp top-LEFT (see stamp rule note) |
| Concept 3 — 1:1 | `c3_square_1080x1080.png` | 1080x1080, sRGB PNG | As concept 1 |
| Editable source | `../compose_5388.py` | — | Fully parametric Python/PIL compose script (the layout system); re-run reproduces all 9 finals deterministically |

## How the pipeline worked

- **Two source frames per concept, as supplied**: the vertical master (`concept{i}_photo_tall.jpg`)
  feeds the 9:16 and 4:5 crops; the square alternate frame (`concept{i}_photo_sq.jpg`) is the 1:1
  base — no substituted imagery.
- **Adobe connector element processing (13 ops, all requestIds in trajectory.json)**:
  `image_remove_background` on the stamp (1), `image_apply_auto_tone` on all 6 supplied photos,
  `image_crop_and_resize` with a both-people focus prompt for the 6 story/feed crops. Every crop
  was visually checked: both people present in all 9 finals.
- **Local composition (PIL)**: the connector is an element processor; multi-element layout
  (scrim, teal rule, type, CTA chip, stamp) was assembled locally by `compose_5388.py`. All
  headline / supporting line / CTA label strings are read programmatically from
  `input_assets/ad_copy.json` and rendered verbatim — verified byte-identical (27/27 strings).

## Brand-rule compliance

- **Palette**: teal `#3AA8A0` (accent rule), purple `#6B4FA1` (stamp, untouched), gold `#F2B23E`
  on the CTA chip ONLY (pixel-scan verified: zero gold pixels outside the chip on all 9 finals),
  ink `#232A31` (scrim + chip label), off-white `#FAF6EF` (headline/support).
- **Stamp rule**: 8% of canvas width (86px), clearspace >= half its diameter on all sides, never
  recoloured, never enlarged. Placed top-right by default; flipped to top-LEFT on two files where
  the right corner would break the never-over-a-face rule: `c2_square` (wall photos containing a
  child's face) and `c3_feed` (teen's head reaches the corner).
- **Hierarchy** (phone-checked at 25% zoom / 270px thumbnails): visual -> headline -> supporting
  line -> CTA chip -> stamp. Stories keep the top 220px and bottom 200px free of critical content.
- **Typography**: Avenir Next Demibold headlines (92/84/76px by format), Medium support at 92%
  off-white, Bold 40px chip label — contemporary humanist per the brand guide's intent (see
  limitations).

## Honest limitations

- **Source resolution**: supplied photography is 1024-class (1024x1536 / 1024x1024). Meeting the
  exact 1080px spec required a small upscale — 1.25x for stories (864x1536 -> 1080x1920) and
  1.05x for feed/square. No further upscaling was done or claimed.
- **Fonts**: brand_guide.md names Libre Franklin (headlines) and Lora (support); these are not
  installed on this machine and the binding plan specified Avenir Next (demibold/medium/bold) as
  the contemporary-humanist equivalent. Swapping faces is a one-line change in `compose_5388.py`.
- **Scrim depth**: the planned ink scrim (~38% of canvas height, ~42% square) was extended upward
  on the longer-copy creatives (up to ~63% on c1 feed) so the measured text block always sits on
  a smooth 0 -> 88% ramp — logged per-creative in the trajectory.
- **Shrink-to-fit**: c1's 46-character headline rendered at 64px (not 76px) on the square so the
  text block stays below the mother's face; all other finals use the planned sizes.
- **Editable source**: no Canva/Figma/PSD licence in this pipeline — the editable source supplied
  is the parametric `compose_5388.py` (plus `work/compose_manifest.json` with every measurement);
  it regenerates all 9 finals exactly.

input_assets.what_it_is: The client-approved copy deck: per concept a headline, supporting line, CTA label, photo brief and mood words — the verbatim source for every string rendered on the 9 finals.

items.question: Does the deck contain exactly three concepts mapped to the briefed angles in order — id 1 'Same old dynamic' / 'recognition-of-the-pattern', id 2 'Growing silent gaps' / 'the-quiet-distance-at-home', id 3 'A new framework' / 'a-real-way-forward' — each with a headline, ONE supporting line and a cta_label?
items.guidance: Open ad_copy.json and check ids, names and angle slugs against the three angles named in TASK.md (recognition of the pattern / the quiet distance at home / a real way forward). ad_copy.md should be the identical JSON in a fenced block, not a divergent rewrite.
items.reference: Yes — all three concepts present in that order, each with headline / supporting_line / cta_label; ad_copy.md mirrors the JSON exactly.

items.question: Rate the copy against the persona — an exhausted, relationship-driven mother who is done with tips and wants a system. Consider 'Tired of the same conversations, same results' (7 words), 'The quiet has grown louder between you' (7 words), 'Beyond tips: a system for real change at home' (9 words), and the CTAs 'Explore a new way' / 'Discover the path' / 'See the system'.
items.guidance: 5 = every headline is at or under 9 words, dignified and specific, zero clickbait, exclamation marks or emojis; supporting lines are at or under 15 words and bridge recognition to the landing page; CTAs are 2-4 calm words with 'see how it works' energy. 3 = on-brief but generic enough to fit any parenting brand. 1 = clickbait devices, fear-mongering, or therapy-clinic jargon.
items.reference: Strong pass expected (4-5): all headlines fit the 9-word cap, there are no exclamation marks anywhere, and concept 3 explicitly contrasts 'tips' with 'a system' — the persona's stated need.

items.question: Transcribe concept 2's three renderable strings (headline, supporting_line, cta_label) exactly as they appear in ad_copy.json.
items.guidance: Copy character-for-character including punctuation — these are the strings the finals must reproduce byte-identically, so this transcription is the answer key for the c2 output checks.
items.reference: Headline: 'The quiet has grown louder between you' — Supporting: 'This unspoken distance feels familiar. There is a path to reconnect.' — CTA: 'Discover the path'.

input_assets.what_it_is: One-page brand guide the designer must execute from: 5-colour palette with roles, stamp usage rule, mobile-first type hierarchy, do/don't list, and the CTA chip spec.

items.question: Rate the guide as a working document a freelance designer could execute from without follow-up questions.
items.guidance: 5 = the palette table gives all five exact hexes with unambiguous roles (Soft Teal #3AA8A0 calm fields, Purple #6B4FA1 wordmark + stamp, Warm Gold #F2B23E 'CTA chips ONLY', Deep Ink #232A31 headline text, Warm Off-White #FAF6EF light fields), a measurable stamp rule (7-9% of canvas width, clearspace at least half the stamp's diameter, never over a face), a 5-step reading order, and a concrete chip spec (gold chip, deep ink label, 2-4 calm words, exactly one per creative). 3 = rules present but some are unmeasurable. 1 = vague mood-board language.
items.reference: The guide contains all of the above verbatim — a 5 unless the expert finds a genuine ambiguity.

items.question: Does the guide mandate Libre Franklin for headlines and Lora for the supporting line — i.e. fonts the finished ads did NOT use (the finals use Avenir Next, disclosed in outputs/README.md)?
items.guidance: Check section 3 'Mobile-First Type Hierarchy'. This establishes that the font substitution in the finals is a traceable deviation from this input document, not an invention by the rubric.
items.reference: Yes — 'Headline (Libre Franklin)' and 'ONE short supporting line (Lora)' are both named in section 3.

input_assets.what_it_is: 1024x1024 brand stamp on white — a purple ring with the 'TeenTalk' wordmark inside — cut out by the connector and placed small in the top corner of all 9 finals.

items.question: Does the wordmark read exactly 'TeenTalk' — one word, capital T in 'Teen' and capital T in 'Talk', modern sans-serif — with no tagline or extra glyphs anywhere, and only purple and white used (no gradients, shadows or bevels)?
items.guidance: Check the letterforms one by one; AI logo generators frequently double letters, warp the second word, or sneak in decorations. Confirm the flat-vector look the generation prompt demanded.
items.reference: Yes — clean 'TeenTalk' in purple (#6B4FA1-class) on white, flat, no extra elements.

items.question: Rate the stamp's production usability for this job: cutout-friendly plain white background, even ring weight, clean circular geometry, sturdy letterforms with generous clearspace from the ring.
items.guidance: 5 = a perfectly centred circle with smooth edges that survive background removal cleanly, the wordmark well clear of the ring. 3 = minor wobble in the circle or tight wordmark clearspace. 1 = ragged or distorted geometry that would show even at 86px placement size.
items.reference: The supplied stamp is clean, symmetrical, evenly weighted with generous clearspace — high score expected; the connector cutout in work/ shows no edge artefacts.

items.question: Pipeline QC flagged this badge as 'a ring, not a filled round badge as required' (it still vision-scored 9). At the placed size — 86px wide, 8% of a 1080px canvas, over photography — rate whether the open-ring form stays legible and brand-credible.
items.guidance: Look at any final's corner stamp (e.g. top-right of c1_story_1080x1920.png): at 86px the wordmark is only a few pixels tall and the white interior disc is what keeps the purple ring visible against photos. 5 = it reads instantly as a brand mark even though the wordmark is unreadable at that size; 3 = the ring is visible but the interior reads as an empty hole; 1 = the mark disappears into the photograph.
items.reference: Honest mid-to-high score: the purple ring plus white disc read clearly on all 9 finals, but 'TeenTalk' itself is illegible at 86px — a filled badge would not have fixed the wordmark size.

input_assets.what_it_is: Two gpt-image-2 frames of the concept-1 late-afternoon kitchen scene: a 1024x1536 vertical master (source of the 9:16 and 4:5 crops) and a 1024x1024 wider alternate shot from across the room (the 1:1 base).

items.question: Rate photographic realism across both frames: the mother's coily tied-back hair with flyaway strands, her skin texture and necklace, the teen's hands and thumbs on his phone, the dish-rack clutter and white mug, and the natural window light.
items.guidance: Zoom in on: the mother's hand resting on the counter (finger count and joints), the dish-rack jumble left of her in the tall frame; in the square frame check the pendant lamps, the stove/range hood, the fruit basket on the counter and the blurred sofa cushions in the foreground. 5 = passes as a candid 35mm documentary photo; 3 = believable at feed size but with tells on close inspection; 1 = CGI gloss, painted hair or anatomical errors.
items.reference: Both frames passed pipeline vision QC at 10/10; expect a 4-5, with any nit-picks most likely in the dish rack and the background appliance shapes.

items.question: Do both frames match the approved photo brief: late-afternoon kitchen, mother (40s) by the counter looking towards her teen, the teen scrolling on a phone with his back slightly turned, her expression quietly weary, the teen absorbed and oblivious?
items.guidance: Check the staging (who is where, phone in hand, back turned) and the emotional register — weary and reflective, not angry; no tears or conflict imagery, per the generation constraints.
items.reference: Yes — tall frame: the mother leans on the counter gazing past the camera with the hooded teen on his phone behind her; square frame: the same dynamic from across the room with the teen standing by the table at right.

items.question: The square frame was generated as 'same home, same two people, same clothes, same light' as the vertical master. List every wardrobe/prop continuity break you can find between the two frames.
items.guidance: Compare the mother's top, the teen's hoodie and trousers, and any held objects across the two files.
items.reference: Mother: olive-green V-neck tee leaning on the counter (tall) vs grey long-sleeve sweater holding a teal mug (square). Teen: slate-blue hoodie with dark striped track pants (tall) vs pale-grey hoodie with jeans (square). The 'same clothes' instruction was not honoured — this matters because the square final runs in the same campaign as the story/feed finals of the same concept.

input_assets.what_it_is: Two frames of the concept-2 evening sofa scene: a vertical master (mother reading at one end, daughter in headphones at the other, warm lamp light) and an over-the-mother's-shoulder square alternate.

items.question: Rate fidelity to the 'Distant, observant, quiet, longing' brief: bodies close on one sofa but worlds apart, the mother's gaze gentle and non-intrusive, warm lamp light, no distress or tears.
items.guidance: In the tall frame the mother should be glancing from her open paperback towards the daughter — not staring — while the daughter is sealed off by over-ear headphones and a device. In the square frame the over-the-shoulder composition should put the viewer literally in the mother's seat, her blurred hair/shoulder and book in the foreground. 5 = the gap is palpable and dignified; 3 = two people merely coexisting with no readable relationship; 1 = staged, melodramatic or distressed.
items.reference: Both frames land the brief: the tall frame catches her mid-glance over the book; the square adopts her exact viewpoint. Expect 4-5.

items.question: Rate realism with attention to the one defect pipeline QC logged on the tall frame: the paperback's pages 'appear slightly thick and uniform'. Also check the daughter's over-ear headphone geometry, her hands on the device, and hair strands against the lamp light.
items.guidance: Zoom on the open book block in both frames (page edges should vary, not look like a moulded slab), the headphone band and cup alignment on the daughter's head, and the mother's bun and earring. 5 = candid and printable; 3 = the book or headphones look slightly moulded but pass at feed size; 1 = plastic skin or warped anatomy.
items.reference: Tall frame QC'd 9/10 with the book-pages issue as the only flag; square frame 10/10. An honest 4 with the book block as the named tell.

items.question: In the square frame, what hangs high on the wall above the sofa, and why did it matter for the final 1:1 layout?
items.guidance: Look at the upper portion of concept2_photo_sq.jpg, especially toward the top-right corner; then cross-check the stamp-rule note for c2_square in outputs/README.md.
items.reference: A cluster of small pinned photo prints containing tiny figures/faces sits high on the wall toward the top-right — they are why the TeenTalk stamp was flipped to the top-LEFT corner on c2_square_1080x1080.png (the never-over-a-face rule).

input_assets.what_it_is: Two frames of the concept-3 morning-kitchen scene: a vertical master (silver-haired mother warmly watching her teen son cut fruit) and a wider square alternate at the marble kitchen island.

items.question: Rate how well these frames deliver the 'real way forward' payoff that must differentiate concept 3 from the distance of concepts 1-2: morning light, purposeful breakfast prep, and the subtle shared ease between the mother (about 50, silver-streaked hair) and her son.
items.guidance: The connection must read as ease, not a staged grin: in the tall frame, her warm sidelong look at the focused teen; in the square, the mirrored soft smiles across the island. 5 = hopeful and credible, a clearly different emotional register from concepts 1-2; 3 = pleasant but interchangeable; 1 = the 'perfect family' stock feel the brief explicitly bans.
items.reference: Both frames show soft mutual ease without grinning — the tall frame is the stronger emotional image. Expect 4-5.

items.question: Rate realism at the classic AI danger spots: the teen's two hands working a knife through apple slices on the wooden board, the fruit (bananas, apples, citrus, the cut pieces), the mother's silver-streaked hair strands, and in the square frame the marble island top and pendant lamp.
items.guidance: Knife-and-hands is the canonical generation failure: count fingers, check the blade's contact with fruit and board, check the cut-fruit geometry. Also inspect the fruit-bowl pile-up at lower-left of the tall frame and the plate of fruit on the island in the square.
items.reference: Both frames QC'd 10/10; the knife grip in the tall frame is plausible (left hand steadying the fruit, right hand on the handle). Expect 4-5.

items.question: Does the tall master keep usable quiet negative space for the ad overlay — i.e. is roughly the top third soft blank wall/window with no critical detail, so the 9:16 crop can hold a corner stamp and clear the Stories UI zone?
items.guidance: The story and feed finals put a multi-line headline low and the stamp high; the source needs croppable headroom above the two heads.
items.reference: Yes — the upper portion of concept3_photo_tall.jpg is plain white wall plus a soft window edge at left; both faces sit in the middle band of the frame.

outputs.display: Concept 1 finals — 'Same old dynamic' (story, feed, square)
outputs.asked: Concept 1 ('recognition of the pattern') delivered in all three required formats: 9:16 1080x1920 Stories/Reels, 4:5 1080x1350 Feed, 1:1 1080x1080 Square.

items.question: On all three files, is the copy byte-exact to ad_copy.json concept 1 — headline 'Tired of the same conversations, same results', supporting line 'You've tried everything. A different approach can change the pattern.', CTA chip 'Explore a new way' — with no other text on the canvas besides the stamp's tiny wordmark, and exactly ONE gold chip per file?
items.guidance: Read every word on each file. Check the comma after 'conversations', the apostrophe in 'You've', and the closing period of the supporting line. Any retyped, truncated or paraphrased string fails this item.
items.reference: Yes on all three — the run claims 27/27 strings verified byte-identical; visual reading confirms the three strings and a single chip per file.

items.question: Rate the text-block craft across the three formats: the short teal accent rule above the headline, headline wrap (3 lines on story and feed; 2 lines at the disclosed 64px shrink-to-fit on the square), supporting line within 2 lines, single gold chip with ink label, no clipping and no text touching a face.
items.guidance: Check the consistent left margin (72px), even line spacing, whether the headline block stays below the mother's face (the square shrink to 64px was done precisely to keep it there), and that the chip clears the bottom edge. On the story format, the top ~220px and bottom ~200px should hold no critical content (stamp excepted) for platform UI overlays.
items.reference: 5 = clean, aligned block with consistent geometry on all three; deduct if the 64px square headline feels undersized next to the 40px chip label, or if the scrim's upper edge cuts visibly across the mother's body.

items.question: Are the three files exactly 1080x1920 (story), 1080x1350 (feed) and 1080x1080 (square) pixels?
items.guidance: Verify actual pixel dimensions of the PNGs, not just the filenames.
items.reference: Yes — confirmed from the PNG headers: 1080x1920, 1080x1350, 1080x1080.

items.question: Rate concept 1's fitness for the warm retargeting audience: does the weary-mother / phone-absorbed-son dynamic survive the crops and the dark ink scrim, and does the pairing with 'Tired of the same conversations, same results' nail the recognition-of-the-pattern angle?
items.guidance: Both people must be present and readable in all three files (the teen's phone may be edge-clipped on the story — disclosed in the trajectory). Judge whether the scrim — extended to roughly 59-70% of canvas height on this concept versus the planned 38/42% — leaves enough of the photograph's emotional information, especially the mother's face and gaze. 5 = instantly stops the right mother; 3 = the faces carry it but the lower photo is lost to the scrim; 1 = it reads as a text card with a sliver of photo.
items.reference: Both people present on all three; the mother's face stays clear of text on each. A 4 is a reasonable anchor — the main deduction is the scrim swallowing most of her body on story and feed.

outputs.display: Concept 2 finals — 'Growing silent gaps' (story, feed, square)
outputs.asked: Concept 2 ('the quiet distance at home') delivered in the same three required formats: 1080x1920, 1080x1350, 1080x1080.

items.question: On all three files, is the copy byte-exact to ad_copy.json concept 2 — headline 'The quiet has grown louder between you', supporting line 'This unspoken distance feels familiar. There is a path to reconnect.', CTA chip 'Discover the path'?
items.guidance: Read every word; the supporting line is two sentences — confirm both periods. Confirm exactly one gold chip per file and no stray text.
items.reference: Yes on all three — strings render verbatim, two-line headline, two-line support, one chip each.

items.question: Rate stamp-rule compliance across this set: roughly 86px (8% of width) purple ring with clearspace of at least half its diameter, placed top-RIGHT on the story and feed but flipped top-LEFT on the square specifically to avoid the pinned wall photos containing small faces.
items.guidance: Eyeball the ~48px offsets from both edges. On c2_square confirm the top-right region indeed shows the wall prints from the source photo and that the stamp sits top-left, over neither the daughter nor the prints. Confirm the stamp is never recoloured or enlarged and never over a face on any of the three; on the feed it sits near the warm lamp — check it still separates from the background.
items.reference: Compliant: story and feed top-right over dim wall/doorway areas, square top-left; the flip decision matches what is genuinely in the photograph. 5 unless clearspace or contrast issues are found.

items.question: On c2_square the mother exists only as a blurred over-the-shoulder foreground (hair, shoulder, book) that the ink scrim further darkens. Rate whether the headline's 'between you' two-person framing still reads, or whether the square has effectively become a single-subject portrait of the headphoned daughter.
items.guidance: View the file at phone size (~270px wide): can a casual viewer tell there is a second person in the frame? The scrim starts around y=298 (covering ~72% of the canvas), swallowing the book and most of the mother's shoulder. 5 = her presence is subtle but unmistakable even at thumbnail size; 3 = discoverable only on inspection; 1 = she is gone and the copy loses its anchor.
items.reference: An honest middle score (around 3): her blurred hair and shoulder survive at the left edge above the scrim, but at thumbnail size she is easy to miss — the weakest two-person read of the nine finals.

items.question: List every gold/amber element you can see across the three c2 files besides the CTA chip, and state whether any of them reads as the flat brand gold #F2B23E of the chip.
items.guidance: Candidates to inspect: the warm table-lamp glow (present in all three), and the mustard throw pillow behind the daughter in the square. The brand rule is gold on the CTA chip ONLY — photographic warm tones are acceptable provided they do not read as the flat brand gold.
items.reference: The lamp glow and the mustard cushion are the only warm-gold elements; both are photographic, desaturated and shadowed, and neither matches the flat #F2B23E chip. Verdict: rule respected (the run's pixel scan reported zero brand-gold pixels outside the chips).

outputs.display: Concept 3 finals — 'A new framework' (story, feed, square)
outputs.asked: Concept 3 ('a real way forward') delivered in the same three required formats: 1080x1920, 1080x1350, 1080x1080.

items.question: On all three files, is the copy byte-exact to ad_copy.json concept 3 — headline 'Beyond tips: a system for real change at home', supporting line 'Move past isolated advice. Explore a structured approach designed for your family dynamic.', CTA chip 'See the system'?
items.guidance: Check the colon after 'Beyond tips' and both sentences of the supporting line. Confirm exactly one gold chip per file.
items.reference: Yes on all three — verbatim, with the headline at 3 lines on the story and 2 lines on feed and square.

items.question: On c3_feed the stamp was flipped top-LEFT because the teen's head reaches the top-right corner. Rate the crop and stamp decisions across the set: story and square keep the stamp top-right over blank wall, the feed flips it, no face is ever covered, and both people stay fully in frame in all three files.
items.guidance: On the feed, check the teen's curly hair against the top-right corner — would a top-right stamp have overlapped it? (It would.) On the story, confirm the top ~220px holds only white wall and the stamp, clear of the Stories UI zone, and that neither the mother's nor the teen's head is cropped in any format.
items.reference: Feed: yes — the teen's head occupies the upper-right, so the flip was necessary and correct; story and square top-right corners are blank wall. Both people are present and uncropped in all three. High score expected.

items.question: On c3_square the wide kitchen-island frame makes both people small, and the first headline line 'Beyond tips: a system for' passes close to the mother's head. Rate the square's layout: face-to-text clearance, mobile legibility of the 76px headline over the busy kitchen, and whether the two people still register as the emotional subject.
items.guidance: Check whether any glyph touches the mother's head or hair (it should clear it, but this is the tightest spacing of the nine files). Check the two-sentence supporting line stays readable over the island clutter thanks to the scrim (which starts around y=298, ~72% of the canvas). At ~270px width, can you still find both faces and read the relationship?
items.reference: No actual collision — the headline clears her head — but this is the tightest face-text spacing and the weakest emotional read of the set because the figures are small. A 3-4 is honest.

items.question: Rate whether the c3 set delivers the 'a real way forward' payoff against concepts 1 and 2: morning brightness, mutual ease at the counter, and copy that promises a system ('Beyond tips… See the system'), giving the three-concept campaign a readable arc from recognition to distance to a way forward.
items.guidance: Put one file from each concept side by side: c1 and c2 should feel like the problem (low light, distance, screens), c3 like the resolution (brighter, warmer, working together). 5 = the arc is unmistakable even though all nine share one layout system; 3 = c3 is brighter but the uniform dark scrim mutes the turn; 1 = the concepts are emotionally indistinguishable.
items.reference: The source photography carries the turn (morning light, shared ease, fruit-cutting); the shared dark-scrim template dampens it somewhat. A 4 is a reasonable anchor.

task_level.question: Did the freelancer deliver exactly what the brief priced: 9 final image files — three concepts each at 1080x1920 (9:16), 1080x1350 (4:5) and 1080x1080 (1:1) — with the correct copy mapped to the correct concept on every file?
task_level.guidance: Count the PNGs in outputs/, verify each file's true pixel dimensions, and spot-check the mapping: every c1 file must carry 'Tired of the same conversations, same results', every c2 file 'The quiet has grown louder between you', every c3 file 'Beyond tips: a system for real change at home'.
task_level.reference: Yes — 9 PNGs at exact spec dimensions (verified from PNG headers) with correct copy-to-concept mapping on all nine.

task_level.question: Rate the nine files as one campaign family: identical layout grammar (teal accent rule, bottom-left text block at a 72px margin, gold chip of constant 96px height, small corner stamp), a consistent type system, and consistent scrim treatment — while the three concepts remain visually distinct.
task_level.guidance: Tile all nine files. 5 = obviously one brand's campaign, with concept identity carried by the photography and copy; 3 = coherent but monotonous — every file is the same dark-bottom template; 1 = geometry drifts between formats. Also weigh the continuity wrinkle: within each concept the square final shows the family in different clothes than the story/feed finals (e.g. concept 1's mother wears an olive tee in story/feed but a grey sweater in the square), because the two source frames broke the 'same clothes' instruction.
task_level.reference: The layout system is rigorously consistent across all nine. The open judgment calls are template monotony and the wardrobe discontinuity between formats of the same concept.

task_level.question: The README discloses four limitations: the editable source is a parametric Python/PIL script (compose_5388.py) rather than the requested Canva/Figma/Photoshop/Illustrator file ('if possible' in the brief); fonts are Avenir Next instead of the brand guide's Libre Franklin (headlines) and Lora (support); the 1024-class AI source photos were upscaled 1.05-1.25x to reach the 1080px spec; and the ink scrims were extended well past the planned 38/42% of canvas (up to ~72% on squares). Rate whether this disclosure package is acceptable for a $120 engagement with one revision round.
task_level.guidance: Judge as the client. 'If possible' softens the source-file ask, but a Python script is unusable to a typical designer; the font swap contradicts the client's own brand guide and is a legitimate revision-round request (both mandated fonts are free Google Fonts); the upscale is modest and invisible at feed size; the deeper scrims are an aesthetic trade for guaranteed legibility. 5 = all deviations reasonable AND honestly flagged; 3 = acceptable only because the included revision round can fix the fonts; 1 = material spec breaches dressed up as disclosures.
task_level.reference: Everything is honestly disclosed and traceable to the trajectory; the font substitution is the deviation most likely to be rejected since Libre Franklin and Lora were explicitly named and freely available.

task_level.question: Across all nine finals, is the flat brand gold #F2B23E used exclusively on the single CTA chip per creative — no gold rules, gold text, gold stamp tints or other gold UI anywhere?
task_level.guidance: Scan each file for any designed element in gold besides the chip; the accent rule must be teal #3AA8A0 and the stamp purple. Photographic warm content (the c2 lamp and mustard cushion, c3's bananas and oranges, wood tones) does not count against the rule.
task_level.reference: Yes — exactly one gold chip per file and no other gold design elements; consistent with the run's pixel-scan claim of zero brand-gold pixels outside the chips.

task_level.question: Acting as TeenTalk's growth lead with a $120 fixed budget and one included revision round: rate the likelihood you would accept this delivery (as-is or after the included revision) rather than dispute it.
task_level.guidance: Weigh for: all 9 files at exact spec, copy rendered verbatim, brand palette and stamp rules honoured with photo-aware corner flips, emotional on-persona photography, honest documentation. Weigh against: wrong typefaces versus the brand guide, scrims burying 52-72% of the photography, the c2 square's near-invisible mother, the c3 square's small figures, no conventional editable source file. 5 = accept as-is and rehire; 4 = accept after the revision round; 3 = accept reluctantly; 2 = demand rework beyond one round; 1 = dispute.
task_level.reference: A reasonable expert lands at 4: the delivery is complete, professional and honest, with the font swap and scrim depth as the natural revision-round requests.

task_level.question: Name the single biggest weakness of this delivery — the one thing most likely to come back as the client's revision request — and justify your pick in one or two sentences.
task_level.guidance: Pick ONE. Candidates worth weighing: (a) Avenir Next used instead of the mandated Libre Franklin/Lora; (b) ink scrims covering 52-72% of the canvas, darkening the emotional photography the brief leads with; (c) wardrobe continuity breaks between the tall and square source frames within each concept; (d) the stamp's 'TeenTalk' wordmark being illegible at the 86px placement size.
task_level.reference: Most defensible: (a) the font substitution — it contradicts an explicit input document (brand_guide.md section 3), is trivially fixable with free fonts, and is exactly the kind of brief-compliance issue the acceptance criteria say must be corrected in the revision round. (b) scrim depth is the strongest aesthetic alternative.

tasks.title: Tri-Fold Menu Design for Traditional Pub
tasks.one_line_task: Design three coordinated tri-fold drinks menus — Main Drinks, Wine List and Cocktails — for a premium British pub, typesetting all 104 supplied priced items exactly.
tasks.one_line_outputs: Three print-ready tri-fold menus: outside and inside spreads as hi-res PNGs plus a two-page print PDF each, sharing one brand family.
tasks.brief:
PROJECT: THE GEORGE INN OUNDLE – MENU DESIGN

I need a professional tri-fold (3-panel foldable) menu design created in Canva for The George Inn, Oundle.

The design should be fully editable in Canva so that we can easily update prices, add or remove products, and print new copies in the future without requiring further design work.

DELIVERABLES

1. Main Drinks Menu (Tri-Fold)
Draught Beer & Cider
Bottled Beer & Cider
Gin
Vodka
Rum
Whisky & Bourbon
Brandy & Cognac
Tequila, Shots & Liqueurs
Soft Drinks
Mixers
Juices

2. Wine List (Tri-Fold)
White Wines
Rosé Wines
Red Wines
Premium Wines
Sparkling Wines
Prosecco
Port
Wine by the Glass & Bottle Pricing

3. Cocktail Menu (Tri-Fold)

Signature Cocktails
Classic Cocktails
Spritz Selection
Mocktails
Premium Serves
Seasonal Specials

DESIGN REQUIREMENTS
 Use The George Inn Oundle logo provided.
Traditional premium British pub/gastro pub style.
Elegant and timeless design.
Suitable for table presentation and printing.
Consistent branding across all three menus.
Professional typography with clear pricing.
High readability.
Print-ready layout.
Include page margins and bleed considerations.
Canva editable source files must be supplied.
All fonts and elements used must be available within Canva.
Design should be easy to update in future.

COLOUR PALETTE
Traditional pub styling.
Cream/parchment background.
Dark green accents.
Black text.
Optional subtle gold highlights.

FILES REQUIRED
Canva editable link/file.
Print-ready PDF versions.
High-resolution PNG exports.

The final design should feel similar to a premium countryside pub, gastro pub, or boutique inn rather than a modern nightclub or chain restaurant.

tasks.readme:
# The George Inn, Oundle — Three Coordinated Tri-Fold Drinks Menus (task 1559)

One family, one look, three documents: parchment cream `#F3ECD9`, dark green
`#1E3B2A`, near-black `#161412`, sparing gold `#B08D3C`; Hoefler Text tracked-caps
mastheads and section heads, Copperplate Light for "OUNDLE", Baskerville
semibold/regular/italic for items, prices and details. Every menu name, section
name, item, detail line and price was read programmatically from
`input_assets/drink_menus.json` and rendered verbatim (verified: 104/104 items,
166/166 price strings — see `work/render_log.json`).

## Panel order (consistent across all three menus)

- **OUTSIDE spread, left → right = [ flap | back | cover ]** — the cover sits on
  the right outside panel, the standard roll-fold rack presentation; the back
  panel is the centre; the flap (outside-left) folds inward.
- **INSIDE spread, left → right = [ panel 1 | panel 2 | panel 3 ]**, continuous
  reading order.
- Sections flow in exact JSON order across the four content panels:
  **flap → inside 1 → inside 2 → inside 3.**

## Geometry

Each spread is **3579 × 2551 px = 303 × 216 mm at 300 dpi** (A4 landscape
297 × 210 mm trim centred, 3 mm bleed all round). Three equal 99 mm panels; fold
lines at trim_x + 99 mm and + 198 mm carry a near-invisible 12% green guide.
All content sits ≥ 8 mm inside the trim and ≥ 6 mm clear of each fold
(programmatically scanned, every spread).

## Asked → produced

| Asked (TASK.md) | Produced | Pixels / dpi | How |
|---|---|---|---|
| Main Drinks Menu tri-fold (11 sections, exact order) | `main-drinks_outside_spread.png`, `main-drinks_inside_spread.png` | 3579×2551 @300dpi each | Connector-processed elements (crest cutout, auto-toned + 4:3-cropped interior, lifted parchment) + local auto-flow composition; Gin and Whisky & Bourbon split at item boundaries with "(continued)" |
| Main Drinks print-ready PDF (margins + bleed) | `main-drinks_print.pdf` | 2 pages, 3679×2651 slug, crop marks at 297×210 trim | Assembled locally with PIL (the connector has no image→PDF tool) |
| Wine List tri-fold (8 sections, exact order) | `wine-list_outside_spread.png`, `wine-list_inside_spread.png` | 3579×2551 @300dpi each | 7 wine sections height-balanced across panels; the 8th, "Wine by the Glass & Bottle Pricing", closes the menu on the back panel beneath the auto-toned exterior |
| Wine List print-ready PDF | `wine-list_print.pdf` | 2 pages, as above | Local PIL assembly, crop marks at trim |
| Cocktail Menu tri-fold (6 sections, exact order) | `cocktails_outside_spread.png`, `cocktails_inside_spread.png` | 3579×2551 @300dpi each | 6 sections balanced with generous leading; firelit interior on the back panel |
| Cocktail Menu print-ready PDF | `cocktails_print.pdf` | 2 pages, as above | Local PIL assembly, crop marks at trim |
| Editable source per menu | `compose_1559.py` (task dir) + `input_assets/drink_menus.json`; supporting vector `support/george_inn_crest.svg` | — | Edit any name/price in the JSON and re-run the script — all six faces regenerate with auto-reflow; the crest SVG (Adobe vectorize API) is editable in Illustrator |

Price columns: spirits carry **25 ml / 50 ml**, wines **175 ml / 250 ml /
Bottle** (premium bins bottle-only; sparkling 125 ml / Bottle; port 50 ml /
Bottle) — column sets and headers are derived from each section's JSON `prices`
keys, values verbatim.

## Honest limitations

- **Crest cutout = connector + local alpha repair.** The Adobe
  `image_remove_background` cutout erased part of the crest's gold lettering
  ("OUNDLE" half-gone, "EST. 1684" removed). Because the original logo sits on
  a flat cream square, the alpha was repaired locally
  (`work/repair_crest.py`): a colour-distance key over the original artwork,
  combined as max(connector mask, key), restores the lettering exactly while
  keeping the connector's clean edges — RGB straight from the client file, no
  recolouring, corners transparent, never boxed in. All three outside spreads
  and print PDFs were re-exported with the repaired crest.
- **No native Canva/InDesign source.** The connector exposes no document-editing
  surface for this layout, so the "editable source" is the JSON + Python
  regeneration pair above (a real no-designer update path), plus the crest SVG.
  A Canva-editable master remains a recorded human-handoff step.
- **PDFs are RGB, assembled locally with PIL** (no connector image→PDF tool).
  They carry crop marks at the 297×210 mm trim with 3 mm bleed; PDF/X-4 /
  CMYK prepress conversion is a print-house step.
- **Parchment texture** ships at 1024×1536 and is scaled up as the full-bleed
  background; under the 78% cream wash this reads as quiet tooth, not detail —
  no sharp-detail asset was upscaled. Crest placed at 620 px from a 1024 px
  cutout; photos placed at 880 px wide from 1536 px originals (all at or below
  native resolution).
- **Microcopy** (the only non-JSON strings on the menus, logged in the
  trajectory): cover branding "THE GEORGE INN" / "OUNDLE" / "Est. 1684", the
  4-line visit block on the Main Drinks and Cocktail back panels (fictional
  01832 phone and URL — replace with the pub's real details before print), and
  "(continued)" on split sections.
- The optional gold coupe glyph on the cocktail cover was skipped (PLAN allowed
  this) — the hairline-rule masthead stays cleaner.
